stem: A developer is building an agent that must decide which tool to call, observe the result, and iterate until a task is done. Which control pattern does the Claude Agent SDK use to drive this?
options: A single Messages API call with all steps hardcoded
An agent loop that repeatedly calls the model, executes returned tool calls, and feeds results back until the model stops requesting tools
A fixed finite-state machine defined entirely in JSON before the run
A batch job that runs all tool calls in parallel without model feedback
rationale: Agentic workflows run a loop: the model requests tool use, the harness executes the tool and returns the result as a tool_result, and the loop continues until the model responds without further tool_use. Options describing hardcoded or single-shot flows are not agentic.
referenceText: Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK

stem: In a workflow that chains several model calls, which approach best improves reliability for a complex multi-step task?
options: Ask for everything in one prompt and accept the first output
Decompose the task into discrete steps (prompt chaining), validating each step's output before the next
Increase max_tokens and temperature to let the model explore
Disable tool use so the model reasons freely
rationale: Prompt chaining decomposes complex work into verifiable steps, improving reliability over a single monolithic prompt. Raising temperature or removing tools does not add reliability.
referenceText: Chain complex prompts for stronger performance

stem: Which field of a Messages API request carries top-level instructions about role and behavior that are not part of the conversation turns?
options: The first user message
The system parameter
A message with role "system"
The metadata parameter
rationale: The Messages API takes a top-level system parameter for system instructions. There is no message with role "system"; messages alternate between user and assistant.
referenceText: Messages API — system prompts

stem: A Messages API request omits max_tokens. What happens?
options: The request succeeds and generates until the model stops
The request fails because max_tokens is required
It defaults to the model's full context window
It defaults to 1 token
rationale: max_tokens is a required parameter on the Messages API; it sets the maximum number of tokens to generate before stopping.
referenceText: Messages API reference

stem: Which are valid, documented ways to reduce cost and/or latency for high-volume Claude API workloads? (Select TWO.)
options: Prompt caching to reuse large, stable prompt prefixes
The Message Batches API for non-time-sensitive requests at reduced cost
Sending each request twice and keeping the faster response
Setting temperature to 0 to shorten outputs
rationale: Prompt caching reuses cached prefixes to cut cost and latency; the Message Batches API processes large asynchronous workloads at reduced cost. Duplicate requests waste spend, and temperature does not control output length.
referenceText: Prompt caching; Message Batches API

stem: To receive tokens incrementally as they are generated rather than waiting for the full response, a developer should:
options: Set stream: true and consume the server-sent events
Poll the Messages API repeatedly for partial output
Use the Batches API
Set max_tokens to a small value
rationale: Setting stream: true streams the response as server-sent events (message_start, content_block_delta, etc.). Batching and polling do not provide incremental streaming.
referenceText: Streaming Messages

stem: After a tool_use response, how must the developer continue the conversation so the model can finish the task?
options: Send a new user message containing a tool_result block referencing the tool_use id
Start a fresh conversation with the tool output in the system prompt
Call a separate /tools endpoint with the result
Append the result to the assistant message and resend it
rationale: The developer executes the tool, then sends the result back as a user message containing a tool_result content block whose tool_use_id matches the model's tool_use block. The model then continues.
referenceText: Tool use — returning results

stem: A team wants Claude to always return output that conforms to a specific JSON structure for a downstream system. Which is the most reliable documented technique?
options: Ask politely for JSON and hope it complies
Define a tool with an input_schema and use tool use to force structured output
Lower max_tokens so only JSON fits
Set temperature to 2.0
rationale: Defining a tool with a JSON input_schema (and optionally tool_choice) makes the model produce structured arguments matching the schema — the documented pattern for reliable structured output.
referenceText: Tool use for structured output

stem: Which file lets a team check project-specific instructions and conventions into a repository so Claude Code applies them automatically?
options: package.json
CLAUDE.md
README.md
.gitignore
rationale: CLAUDE.md holds project memory/instructions Claude Code loads automatically. README and package.json are not Claude Code configuration files.
referenceText: Claude Code — memory & CLAUDE.md

stem: When building an eval to measure whether prompt changes improve quality, what is the most important practice?
options: Test on a single hand-picked example that already works
Use a representative test set with defined success criteria and compare versions against it
Only measure token cost, not output quality
Rely on a one-off manual read of the output
rationale: Reliable evaluation uses a representative dataset and explicit, graded success criteria so prompt or model changes can be compared objectively. Single examples and vibes-based checks do not generalize.
referenceText: Create strong empirical evaluations

stem: A workload needs the highest reasoning quality for complex agentic tasks and cost is secondary. Which model selection principle applies?
options: Always pick the smallest, cheapest model
Choose the most capable model for the hardest tasks, and consider smaller models where they meet quality bars
Model choice never affects quality, only price
Pick a model at random and tune temperature
rationale: Match the model to the task: use the most capable model where quality matters most, and use smaller/faster models when they meet the requirement to optimize cost and latency.
referenceText: Choosing a model

stem: Which capability lets Claude spend additional internal reasoning on hard problems, controllable by a budget?
options: Extended thinking
Prompt caching
Streaming
Stop sequences
rationale: Extended thinking gives the model additional reasoning tokens (with a configurable budget) for complex tasks. Caching, streaming, and stop sequences serve other purposes.
referenceText: Extended thinking

stem: Which are documented prompt-engineering techniques for the Claude API? (Select THREE.)
options: Be clear and direct, stating task, audience, and format
Provide examples (multishot) of the desired input/output
Give Claude a role via the system prompt
Use deliberately vague wording to keep prompts short
rationale: Clear/direct instructions, multishot examples, and role prompting via the system prompt are all documented techniques. Vague wording degrades results.
referenceText: Prompt engineering overview

stem: Placing large, stable reference material near the top of the prompt and reusing it across many requests pairs best with which feature to control cost?
options: Prompt caching
Stop sequences
Temperature
Top-k sampling
rationale: Stable prefixes (e.g., long documents or instructions) are ideal candidates for prompt caching, which reuses the cached prefix across requests to reduce cost and latency.
referenceText: Prompt caching

stem: An application concatenates untrusted user text directly into instructions Claude will follow. What is the primary risk and mitigation?
options: No risk; user text is always safe
Prompt injection; separate and clearly delimit untrusted content and constrain the model's actions/permissions
Latency; enable streaming
Cost; enable batching
rationale: Injecting untrusted content into instructions enables prompt injection. Mitigations include clearly delimiting untrusted input, instructing the model how to treat it, and limiting tool permissions. Streaming and batching are unrelated.
referenceText: Mitigate jailbreaks and prompt injections

stem: What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
options: A proprietary Anthropic-only binary format
An open protocol that standardizes how applications provide tools, resources, and context to LLMs via servers and clients
A prompt template language
A replacement for the Messages API
rationale: MCP is an open standard for connecting models to external tools, data, and context through MCP servers and clients. It is not proprietary, a template language, or an API replacement.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol

stem: Which primitives does an MCP server expose to clients? (Select THREE.)
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Billing plans
rationale: MCP servers expose tools (callable functions), resources (contextual data), and prompts (reusable templates). Billing is not an MCP primitive.
referenceText: MCP server concepts

stem: Which stop_reason indicates the model paused specifically because it wants the developer to run a tool?
options: end_turn
max_tokens
tool_use
stop_sequence
rationale: A stop_reason of tool_use means the response contains one or more tool_use blocks and is waiting for tool results. end_turn is a natural completion; max_tokens and stop_sequence indicate other stops.
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason

stem: In the Messages API, conversation history must be provided as:
options: A single concatenated string
A messages array alternating user and assistant roles
Separate API calls per turn with no history
The system parameter only
rationale: The Messages API takes a messages array of turns that alternate between user and assistant roles; prior turns are resent to maintain context.
referenceText: Messages API

stem: A high-throughput classification job must minimize cost while meeting a modest quality bar. The best optimization is to:
options: Use the largest model regardless of need
Select a smaller/faster model that meets the quality bar and validate with an eval
Raise temperature to speed generation
Disable safety guardrails
rationale: Right-sizing to a smaller/faster model that passes an eval optimizes cost/latency without sacrificing the required quality. Oversizing wastes spend; temperature and guardrails are irrelevant to this trade-off.
referenceText: Reducing latency & cost

stem: You call the Messages API with a `tools` array. Claude responds and you want to know whether it decided to invoke one of your tools before you write handling code. Which top-level field on the response signals that Claude wants a tool executed?
options: `stop_reason` equal to `tool_use`
`stop_reason` equal to `end_turn`
`type` equal to `function_call`
`role` equal to `tool`
rationale: When Claude wants to use a tool, the response `stop_reason` is `tool_use` and the content contains one or more `tool_use` blocks; your code then runs the tool and returns results. `end_turn` means Claude finished normally with no tool call. `function_call` is OpenAI-style terminology, not the Anthropic Messages API. `role` on the assistant response is always `assistant`; there is no `tool` role for responses.
referenceText: When Claude decides to use a tool, it returns a response with stop_reason of tool_use.

stem: You are defining a tool for Claude to call. In what format must the `input_schema` field of a tool definition be written so Claude knows the shape of the arguments?
options: A JSON Schema object describing the parameters
A TypeScript interface serialized as a string
A natural-language description of each argument
A Python function signature with type hints
rationale: The `input_schema` field must be a JSON Schema object (with `type`, `properties`, `required`, etc.) that defines the parameters Claude should supply. A TypeScript interface, a plain prose description, or a Python signature are not accepted formats; the API validates against JSON Schema. Prose belongs in the tool's `description`, not `input_schema`.
referenceText: input_schema defines the expected parameters for the tool as a JSON Schema object.

stem: Your workflow must force Claude to call one specific tool named `get_weather` on the next turn rather than replying with text or picking a different tool. What `tool_choice` value accomplishes this?
options: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}`
`{"type": "any"}`
`{"type": "auto"}`
`{"type": "none"}`
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}` forces Claude to call that exact tool. `any` forces some tool but lets Claude choose which one. `auto` (the default) lets Claude decide whether to use a tool at all. `none` prevents tool use entirely, which is the opposite of the requirement.
referenceText: tool_choice can be set to {type: tool, name: ...} to force use of a specific named tool.

stem: Claude returned a `tool_use` block, your code executed the tool, and now you must return the output to Claude. How do you structure the follow-up request?
options: Add a `user` message containing a `tool_result` block whose `tool_use_id` matches the original `tool_use` block
Add an `assistant` message containing a `tool_result` block with the raw output
Add a `system` message describing the tool output in prose
Resend the same request with the output appended to the tool's `description`
rationale: Tool results are returned in a new `user` message as a `tool_result` content block whose `tool_use_id` matches the `id` of the assistant's `tool_use` block. Results are never placed in an assistant or system message, and they must be structured blocks tied by id, not appended to the tool description.
referenceText: Return tool results in a user message with a tool_result block referencing the matching tool_use_id.

stem: You enabled streaming (`stream: true`) and are consuming server-sent events to display Claude's answer as it is generated. Which SSE event delivers the incremental chunks of generated text?
options: `content_block_delta` events carrying a `text_delta`
`message_start` events carrying the full text
`message_stop` events carrying the completed text
`ping` events carrying partial text
rationale: Incremental text arrives in `content_block_delta` events whose delta is a `text_delta`. `message_start` announces the message shell with empty content, `message_stop` marks the end of the stream, and `ping` events are keep-alives that carry no content.
referenceText: content_block_delta events contain text_delta updates that append text to a content block.

stem: You must classify 500,000 archived support tickets. Latency does not matter, but cost does, and the job can run overnight. Which Anthropic API is the best fit?
options: The Message Batches API, which processes large asynchronous jobs at reduced cost
The streaming Messages API, to get partial results token by token
The Files API, to upload the tickets for automatic labeling
The extended thinking API, to reason longer over each ticket
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume, latency-tolerant workloads, processing batches asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly half the standard token cost. Streaming optimizes for real-time delivery, not throughput or cost. The Files API stores files but does not classify them, and extended thinking increases cost and latency, the opposite of the goal.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at 50% lower cost.

stem: You want to reuse a large, unchanging system prompt across many requests to cut cost and latency with prompt caching. Where should the cached, static content be placed relative to the dynamic, per-request content?
options: Static content first, with `cache_control` marking the end of the cacheable prefix, and dynamic content after it
Dynamic content first, so the newest tokens are cached, then the static content
Static and dynamic content interleaved so each turn caches independently
Dynamic content only; static content cannot be cached
rationale: Prompt caching keys on a prefix, so stable content must come first and the `cache_control` breakpoint marks the end of the cacheable prefix; variable content follows and is not cached. Putting dynamic content first would invalidate the cache every request, and interleaving breaks the contiguous prefix. Static content is exactly what benefits from caching.
referenceText: Place static content at the beginning of the prompt and mark the cache breakpoint with cache_control.

stem: You enable extended thinking on a hard reasoning task. Which parameter controls how many tokens Claude may spend on its internal reasoning before producing the final answer?
options: `thinking.budget_tokens`
`max_tokens` alone
`temperature`
`top_k`
rationale: Extended thinking is configured with a `thinking` object whose `budget_tokens` sets the reasoning token allowance (and must be less than `max_tokens`). `max_tokens` bounds the overall output but does not by itself enable or size the thinking budget. `temperature` and `top_k` control sampling, not reasoning depth.
referenceText: Set thinking with type enabled and budget_tokens to control the reasoning token budget.

stem: You are building a high-volume, cost-sensitive agent step that only needs to route short user messages into one of five categories with very low latency. Based on the models overview, which choice best matches the requirement?
options: A Haiku-class model, optimized for speed and cost on lightweight tasks
An Opus-class model, for maximum reasoning depth on every message
Whichever model has the largest context window regardless of cost
A different model per request chosen at random to balance load
rationale: Haiku-class models are the fastest and most cost-efficient, ideal for simple, high-throughput classification and routing. Opus maximizes intelligence but is slower and costlier than needed here. Context-window size is irrelevant for short messages, and random model selection produces inconsistent quality and cost with no benefit.
referenceText: Haiku models are the fastest and most cost-effective, suited to high-volume, lightweight tasks.

stem: A team wants to build a custom coding agent that reuses the same harness that powers Claude Code, including its agentic loop, tool execution, and permission handling, rather than re-implementing that loop themselves. Which offering is the intended foundation?
options: The Claude Agent SDK
The raw Messages API with a hand-written while-loop only
The Message Batches API
The prompt caching feature
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the same agent harness that underlies Claude Code, giving developers the agentic loop, tool use, context management, and permissions out of the box. The raw Messages API can build an agent but requires re-implementing the loop yourself. Batches and prompt caching are complementary capabilities, not an agent framework.
referenceText: The Claude Agent SDK provides the same infrastructure that powers Claude Code for building custom agents.

stem: Your repository has a `CLAUDE.md` file at its root. In Claude Code, what is the primary purpose of this file?
options: It provides persistent project context and instructions that Claude Code automatically loads into every session
It defines the CI pipeline that runs when Claude Code opens a pull request
It stores the API key Claude Code uses to authenticate
It lists the exact shell commands Claude Code is forbidden from running
rationale: `CLAUDE.md` is Claude Code's memory file: project conventions, architecture notes, and instructions that are automatically pulled into context at the start of a session. It is not a CI config, not a credential store, and not the permissions/deny list (which lives in settings, not CLAUDE.md).
referenceText: CLAUDE.md is automatically loaded as memory to give Claude Code persistent project context.

stem: You frequently ask Claude Code to run the same multi-step review prompt. You want to invoke it by typing `/review-pr`. How do you create this reusable custom command?
options: Add a Markdown file (e.g. `review-pr.md`) in the project's `.claude/commands/` directory containing the prompt
Register the command by adding it to `package.json` scripts
Define it as a shell alias in your `.bashrc`
Encode the prompt into the tool's `input_schema`
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files placed in `.claude/commands/` (project) or `~/.claude/commands/` (personal); the filename becomes the command name and the file body is the prompt. `package.json` scripts and shell aliases are unrelated to Claude Code's command system, and `input_schema` describes tool parameters, not slash commands.
referenceText: Create custom slash commands by adding Markdown files to the .claude/commands directory.

stem: You want Claude Code to automatically run a formatter every time it finishes editing a file, without you asking each time. Which Claude Code feature is designed to trigger deterministic shell commands on such lifecycle events?
options: Hooks
Slash commands
Plan mode
CLAUDE.md memory
rationale: Hooks run user-defined shell commands automatically at defined events (such as PostToolUse after a file edit), making behavior deterministic rather than relying on the model to remember. Slash commands are manually invoked prompts, plan mode is a read-only planning state, and CLAUDE.md is passive context that cannot execute commands.
referenceText: Hooks are shell commands that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle.

stem: Before letting Claude Code modify a large codebase, you want it to research the code and propose a step-by-step approach that you approve first, with no files changed until you accept. Which Claude Code mode is intended for this?
options: Plan mode, which restricts Claude to read-only analysis and produces a plan for approval
Auto-accept mode, which applies every proposed edit immediately
Batch mode, which queues edits for asynchronous processing
Streaming mode, which shows edits token by token as they apply
rationale: Plan mode keeps Claude Code in a read-only posture: it investigates and presents a plan you approve before any changes are made. Auto-accept does the opposite by applying edits without review. There is no 'batch mode' for edits in Claude Code, and streaming concerns output delivery, not a read-only planning guarantee.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude Code analyze and plan without making changes until you approve.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, an MCP server exposes capabilities called 'tools'. Who or what decides when a tool is invoked during an interaction?
options: The model, which chooses to call tools as part of its reasoning (model-controlled)
The end user, who must click each tool to run it (user-controlled)
The host application, which runs tools on a fixed schedule (application-controlled)
The MCP server, which invokes its own tools autonomously
rationale: MCP tools are model-controlled: the language model decides when to call them, analogous to Messages API tool use. Prompts are user-controlled (user-initiated templates) and resources are application-controlled (the app decides how context is supplied). The server exposes tools but does not decide when they run.
referenceText: Tools in MCP are model-controlled, meaning the model decides when to invoke them.

stem: You are designing an MCP server that should expose read-only reference data (files, database rows, documentation) for the client application to load into context as needed, without the model deciding to fetch it. Which MCP primitive fits this role?
options: Resources, which are application-controlled context sources identified by URI
Tools, which the model invokes to perform actions
Prompts, which are user-selected interaction templates
Sampling, which lets the server request completions from the client
rationale: Resources are application-controlled data exposed by URI that the client can read to supply context; they are typically read-only and not invoked by the model. Tools are model-invoked actions, prompts are user-selected templates, and sampling is a server-initiated request for a model completion, none of which match 'application supplies read-only context'.
referenceText: Resources are application-controlled data that clients can read to provide context to the model.

stem: An MCP server offers reusable, parameterized message templates that a user can explicitly select from a menu to start a specific workflow (for example, 'summarize this PR'). Which MCP primitive represents these user-initiated templates?
options: Prompts, which are user-controlled templates surfaced for the user to choose
Resources, which are read-only context data
Tools, which the model calls autonomously
Roots, which define filesystem boundaries
rationale: MCP prompts are user-controlled: predefined, often parameterized templates the user deliberately selects to trigger a workflow. Resources supply context data, tools are model-invoked actions, and roots define the filesystem scope a client grants a server, none of which are user-selected templates.
referenceText: Prompts are user-controlled templates that users can select to guide an interaction.

stem: Your chat agent's second request to the Messages API seems to 'forget' the first exchange. What is the correct explanation and fix given how the API works?
options: The Messages API is stateless, so you must resend the full prior conversation (all user and assistant turns) in the `messages` array each request
The API caches conversations by API key, so you only need to send the newest message and it recalls the rest
You must set a `conversation_id` so the server retrieves stored history automatically
Assistant turns are dropped automatically, so only user turns need to be resent
rationale: The Messages API is stateless: the model only sees what you send, so you must include the entire prior conversation (both user and assistant turns) in the `messages` array on every request. There is no server-side conversation store keyed by API key, no `conversation_id` retrieval, and assistant turns must be included to preserve context.
referenceText: The Messages API is stateless; include the full conversation history in each request.

stem: You are writing a tool definition for the Messages API tool-use feature. Which fields are part of a single tool definition object? (Select 3.)
options: `name`, a unique identifier for the tool
`description`, explaining what the tool does and when to use it
`input_schema`, a JSON Schema for the tool's parameters
`stop_reason`, indicating why generation ended
`tool_use_id`, linking a result back to a call
rationale: A tool definition consists of `name`, `description`, and `input_schema`; a thorough `description` strongly improves tool-use accuracy. `stop_reason` is a field on the model's response, not on a tool definition, and `tool_use_id` appears on `tool_use`/`tool_result` blocks during the exchange, not in the definition you register.
referenceText: Each tool is defined with a name, description, and input_schema.

stem: Your team is deciding between a fixed workflow and a more autonomous agent for a new feature. According to Anthropic's guidance on building effective agents, which statements are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: Workflows orchestrate LLMs and tools through predefined code paths
Agents let the LLM dynamically direct its own process and tool usage
Agents should always be preferred because they are strictly more capable than workflows
Workflows require extended thinking to be enabled to function
Agents eliminate the need to define any tools for the model
rationale: Anthropic distinguishes workflows (LLMs and tools coordinated by predefined code paths) from agents (the LLM dynamically controls its own process and tool use). The other options are false: the guidance recommends the simplest solution that works rather than always choosing agents; workflows do not require extended thinking; and agents still rely on well-defined tools to act.
referenceText: Workflows use predefined code paths; agents dynamically direct their own processes and tool use.

stem: You are defining a tool named get_weather for the Messages API. Which field of the tool definition tells Claude what arguments the tool accepts and constrains how Claude structures the arguments it emits in a tool_use block?
options: input_schema, a JSON Schema object describing the tool's parameters
parameters, an OpenAPI 3.0 path specification
output_schema, a JSON Schema describing the tool's return value
tool_result, a template of the expected response payload
rationale: A tool definition requires name, description, and input_schema. input_schema is a JSON Schema object that defines the parameters and their types; Claude uses it to generate a validly structured tool_use input. 'parameters'/OpenAPI is the naming used by other providers, not Anthropic's Messages API. There is no output_schema field on tool definitions. tool_result is the block you send back with a tool's output, not part of the tool definition.
referenceText: Tool use: each tool has a name, description, and input_schema (a JSON Schema object) that defines the shape of the input Claude must provide.

stem: On the next turn you want to guarantee Claude calls exactly the tool get_weather rather than answering in prose or choosing a different tool. Which tool_choice value accomplishes this?
options: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "auto"}
{"type": "none"}
rationale: tool_choice of type 'tool' with a name forces Claude to invoke that specific tool. 'any' forces the model to use some tool but not a particular one. 'auto' (the default when tools are provided) lets Claude decide whether and which tool to call. 'none' prevents tool use entirely.
referenceText: tool_choice can be auto (model decides), any (must use a tool), tool (must use the named tool), or none (no tools).

stem: Your app sends a message with a tool defined. Claude responds and the API returns stop_reason "tool_use". What is the correct next step to continue the agentic loop?
options: Execute the requested tool in your code, then send a new user-role message containing a tool_result block that references the tool_use_id
Immediately re-send the same request unchanged so Claude retries the tool itself
Send the tool output back as a system prompt so Claude can read it
Return the tool output as an assistant-role message with a tool_use block
rationale: When stop_reason is 'tool_use', your code runs the tool and returns the output in a user-role message as a tool_result block whose tool_use_id matches the request. Re-sending unchanged does not give Claude the result. Tool output does not go in the system prompt. Results are returned in a user message via tool_result, not as an assistant tool_use block (which is what Claude produces, not you).
referenceText: After a tool_use stop reason, run the tool and return a tool_result content block in a user message, matching the tool_use id.

stem: In a single response Claude emits two independent tool_use blocks (one to look up weather, one to look up time). What is the correct way to respond so the conversation continues in one round trip?
options: Execute both tools and return both results as two tool_result blocks inside a single user-role message
Return only the first tool_result and prompt Claude to ask again for the second
Set tool_choice to "any" and resend so Claude picks one tool to run
Return each tool_result in its own separate API request with distinct API keys
rationale: Claude can request multiple tools in parallel within one assistant message; you execute all of them and return all corresponding tool_result blocks in a single following user message. Returning only one result breaks the pairing of tool_use_ids and wastes turns. Changing tool_choice or splitting into separate keyed requests is unnecessary and incorrect.
referenceText: Claude may return multiple tool_use blocks; return all tool_result blocks together in the next user message.

stem: You are streaming a Messages API response (stream=true) that involves tool use. Which streaming event delta carries the incremental JSON for a tool's input as it is generated?
options: content_block_delta events with an input_json_delta
message_delta events with a tool_json_delta
content_block_start events with a text_delta
tool_result_delta events with a partial_json field
rationale: During streaming, a tool_use block's arguments arrive as content_block_delta events whose delta type is input_json_delta, carrying partial JSON that you accumulate. text_delta carries assistant text, not tool input. message_delta carries top-level changes like stop_reason and usage, not tool JSON. There is no tool_result_delta event type.
referenceText: Streaming: tool_use input is delivered via content_block_delta events of type input_json_delta containing partial_json.

stem: While consuming a streamed Messages response, which event is always the first event of the stream and provides the initial Message object with empty content?
options: message_start
content_block_start
message_delta
ping
rationale: A stream begins with a message_start event containing a Message object with empty content. content_block_start opens each individual content block after the message has started. message_delta communicates later top-level changes. ping events may be interspersed for keep-alive but are not the guaranteed first event.
referenceText: Each stream begins with a message_start event, followed by content blocks (content_block_start / delta / stop), then message_delta and message_stop.

stem: A team wants to process 50,000 non-urgent classification prompts overnight at lower cost using the Message Batches API. Which two statements about the Batches API are correct? (Select 2.)
options: It processes requests asynchronously with a target of completing each batch within 24 hours
It offers a cost discount (roughly 50%) compared with standard synchronous requests
It guarantees each individual request returns within one second
It requires streaming (stream=true) to be enabled on every request
It executes requests strictly in submitted order and returns them as a live ordered stream
rationale: The Message Batches API runs asynchronously, targeting completion within 24 hours, and prices batched requests at about a 50% discount. It is explicitly for non-real-time work, so there is no per-request one-second guarantee. Streaming is not required (and is not used for batch requests). Results can be retrieved when the batch finishes and are not guaranteed to be returned in input order as a live stream.
referenceText: Message Batches process large volumes asynchronously within 24 hours at a 50% cost reduction versus standard API calls.

stem: You want to reuse a large, static system prompt across many requests to reduce cost and latency with prompt caching. Which two statements are correct? (Select 2.)
options: You mark a cache breakpoint by adding cache_control with type "ephemeral" to a content block
Cached prefixes must meet a minimum token length to be eligible for caching
Cached content is stored permanently and shared across all organizations
Prompt caching requires submitting the request through the Message Batches API
Enabling caching has no effect on the cost of cache read tokens
rationale: Prompt caching is enabled by adding cache_control (type 'ephemeral') to the content block that ends the cacheable prefix, and the prefix must meet a minimum token threshold to be cached. Caches are short-lived (ephemeral, e.g. a few minutes TTL) and scoped to your organization, not permanent or globally shared. Caching is independent of the Batches API. Cache reads are billed at a reduced rate, so caching does affect cost.
referenceText: Add cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'} to define a cache breakpoint; prefixes must meet a minimum token count and cache reads are discounted.

stem: Your application enables extended thinking on a supported Claude model for a hard reasoning task. Which two statements are correct? (Select 2.)
options: You control how much reasoning Claude can do by setting the thinking budget_tokens parameter
The response includes thinking content blocks that precede the final answer
Extended thinking is not billed as output tokens and is always free
Enabling extended thinking disables tool use for that request
budget_tokens must be set larger than max_tokens for thinking to activate
rationale: Extended thinking is configured with a budget_tokens value that caps the reasoning tokens, and the response contains thinking blocks before the final text. Thinking tokens are billed as output tokens (not free). Extended thinking is compatible with tool use (including interleaved thinking), so it does not disable tools. budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens, not greater.
referenceText: Enable extended thinking by setting a thinking budget_tokens (less than max_tokens); responses include thinking blocks, and thinking tokens are billed.

stem: A workflow must classify millions of short support messages where per-item latency and cost matter far more than deep reasoning. Following Anthropic's model guidance, which family is the most appropriate default choice?
options: A Claude Haiku model, optimized for speed and low cost on high-volume tasks
A Claude Opus model, because the largest model is always the safest default
Whichever model has the largest context window regardless of task
A model with extended thinking forced on for every request to maximize accuracy
rationale: Haiku models are positioned for speed and cost efficiency on high-volume, well-scoped tasks like short-message classification. Defaulting to the largest (Opus) model for a simple, latency-sensitive task wastes cost and adds latency. Context window size is irrelevant for short messages. Forcing extended thinking on every request adds cost and latency without proportional benefit for simple classification.
referenceText: Models overview: Haiku is the fastest, most cost-effective family; Opus is most capable; choose based on task complexity, speed, and cost.

stem: Your team wants to build a production agent that plans, calls tools, reads and edits files, and manages the context loop, reusing the same harness that powers Claude Code. Which Anthropic offering is designed for this?
options: The Claude Agent SDK, which provides the agent loop, tool integration, and context management
The Message Batches API, which turns any prompt into an autonomous agent
Prompt caching, which converts cached prompts into a running agent
The token counting endpoint, which orchestrates multi-step tool use
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the same agent harness behind Claude Code, including the agentic loop, tool use, permissions, and context management, for building custom agents. The Batches API is for asynchronous bulk processing, not agent orchestration. Prompt caching is a cost/latency optimization. Token counting only estimates token usage; it does not run an agent.
referenceText: The Claude Agent SDK gives developers the building blocks (agent loop, tools, context management) used by Claude Code to build custom agents.

stem: In a repository used with Claude Code, what is the primary purpose of a CLAUDE.md file at the project root?
options: To provide persistent project memory/context (conventions, commands, architecture) that Claude automatically loads into each session
To store the API key and model selection used for every request
To define the JSON Schema for each MCP tool the project exposes
To hold the compiled transcript of every previous Claude Code session
rationale: CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's memory file: it captures project conventions, common commands, and architecture notes that are automatically pulled into context so you do not repeat them. It is not a place for secrets/API keys (which belong in environment/config), it is not an MCP tool schema definition, and it is not a session transcript log.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md is loaded as memory at session start to give Claude Code persistent project context such as commands and conventions.

stem: You frequently ask Claude Code to run the same multi-step 'create a release PR' routine. How do you package this as a reusable custom slash command?
options: Create a Markdown prompt file under .claude/commands/ (e.g. release-pr.md); it becomes /release-pr
Add a shell alias in your ~/.bashrc that starts with a slash
Register it as an MCP resource so Claude auto-runs it each turn
Define it inside input_schema of a tool in the Messages API request
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files placed in .claude/commands/ (project) or ~/.claude/commands/ (personal); the filename becomes the command name, e.g. release-pr.md -> /release-pr. A shell alias is unrelated to Claude Code commands. MCP resources are read-only data, not auto-run commands. input_schema defines tool parameters, not slash commands.
referenceText: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands (project) or ~/.claude/commands (personal); the file name becomes the command.

stem: A team wants Claude Code to automatically run a formatter and to block edits to a protected directory. Which two statements about Claude Code hooks are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Hooks run user-defined shell commands at lifecycle events such as PreToolUse and PostToolUse
A PreToolUse hook can block or deny a tool call before it executes
Hooks are natural-language instructions stored in CLAUDE.md that Claude interprets loosely
Hooks can only run once, at the very end of a session
Hooks replace the Messages API by handling all model inference locally
rationale: Hooks are configured shell commands that fire deterministically at defined lifecycle events (e.g., PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop); a PreToolUse hook can return a decision that blocks or denies the pending tool call. They are executable configuration, not loosely interpreted CLAUDE.md prose. They fire at many events, not only at session end. They do not perform model inference or replace the Messages API.
referenceText: Hooks are shell commands that execute at events like PreToolUse and PostToolUse; PreToolUse hooks can block a tool call.

stem: Before letting Claude Code modify a large codebase, you want it to research and propose an approach without making any file edits, then wait for your approval. Which capability fits this need?
options: Plan mode, a read-only mode where Claude analyzes and proposes a plan before executing changes
Streaming mode, which prevents any tool from writing to disk
Batch mode, which queues edits for asynchronous application
tool_choice: none, which permanently disables editing for the repository
rationale: Plan mode restricts Claude Code to read-only investigation so it can present a plan for approval before making edits. Streaming controls how tokens are delivered, not write permissions. There is no 'batch mode' for queuing edits in Claude Code. tool_choice is a Messages API parameter for a single request and does not provide an approval-gated planning workflow.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude Code research and produce a plan without modifying files until you approve it.

stem: You are building an MCP server to expose your app's capabilities to Claude. Which three of the following are the core primitives an MCP server can offer? (Select 3.)
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Model fine-tunes
Embedding indexes
rationale: MCP servers expose three core primitives: Tools (model-invocable actions), Resources (contextual data the host can read), and Prompts (reusable templated interactions). Model fine-tunes and embedding indexes are not MCP primitives; MCP is a protocol for connecting clients and servers, not a training or vector-store service.
referenceText: MCP servers provide three primitives: Tools, Resources, and Prompts.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, what best distinguishes a Resource from a Tool?
options: A Resource exposes read-only contextual data identified by a URI, while a Tool is a model-invocable action that can have side effects
A Resource performs write operations, while a Tool only returns static text
A Resource is a fine-tuned model, while a Tool is a system prompt
A Resource runs on the client and a Tool runs only in the LLM itself
rationale: Resources are application-controlled, typically read-only pieces of context addressed by a URI (like a file or record) that the host can load, whereas Tools are model-controlled actions the model can call to do work, potentially with side effects. The other options invert this or misdescribe MCP: resources are not writes, not fine-tuned models, and tools execute on the server (exposed to the model), not inside the LLM.
referenceText: Resources provide read-only context via URIs (application-controlled); Tools are functions the model can invoke to perform actions.

stem: You are connecting a Claude client to a local MCP server that runs as a subprocess on the same machine. Which transport is the standard choice for this local process-to-process connection?
options: stdio (standard input/output)
SMTP
WebRTC data channels
FTP
rationale: MCP defines stdio transport for local servers launched as subprocesses (communicating over standard input/output) and a streamable HTTP transport for remote servers. SMTP (email), WebRTC, and FTP are not MCP transports.
referenceText: MCP supports stdio transport for local subprocess servers and streamable HTTP for remote servers.

stem: Your multi-turn chat app calls the Messages API. Between turns, the API does not retain any conversation state on the server. To preserve context, what must each new request include?
options: The full prior conversation as an ordered messages array of alternating user and assistant turns
Only the latest user message, since the server remembers previous turns by session id
A conversation_id that points to server-stored history
Nothing extra; the system prompt alone reconstructs prior turns
rationale: The Messages API is stateless: each request must contain the entire relevant conversation history in the messages array (alternating user/assistant roles). There is no server-side session memory, no conversation_id that retrieves stored history, and the system prompt does not reconstruct prior turns. You are responsible for accumulating and resending the history.
referenceText: The Messages API is stateless; include the full sequence of prior user and assistant turns in the messages array on each call.

stem: You are building an agent that, on certain turns, must guarantee Claude responds by calling your `extract_invoice` tool rather than replying with free-form text or picking a different tool. Which `tool_choice` value in the Messages API request accomplishes this?
options: `{"type": "tool", "name": "extract_invoice"}`
`{"type": "any"}`
`{"type": "auto"}`
`{"type": "none"}`
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": "extract_invoice"}` forces Claude to call that specific named tool, which is exactly what is required. `{"type": "any"}` forces Claude to use a tool but lets the model choose which one, so a different tool could be invoked. `{"type": "auto"}` (the default when tools are provided) lets Claude decide whether to use any tool at all, so it may reply with plain text. `{"type": "none"}` prevents the model from using any tools.
referenceText: Tool use overview — forcing tool use with tool_choice

stem: An MCP server team wants to expose a catalog of read-only reference documents (policy PDFs, product specs) so that the host application decides which ones to load into Claude's context, rather than having the model autonomously invoke an action to fetch them. Which Model Context Protocol primitive is designed for this application-controlled, data-providing use case?
options: Resources
Tools
Prompts
Sampling
rationale: Resources expose data/content that the host application chooses to surface as context; they are application-controlled and are meant for read-only reference data like files and documents. Tools are model-controlled: the model decides to invoke them to perform actions or computations. Prompts are user-controlled reusable templates/workflows the user explicitly selects. Sampling is the server-initiated capability to request an LLM completion from the client, not a way to expose reference data.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol — Resources

stem: In Claude Code, a team wants a Prettier formatter to run automatically every time Claude finishes editing a file, without depending on the model remembering to do it. Which Claude Code mechanism is the appropriate way to enforce this deterministic, event-driven behavior?
options: A PostToolUse hook that runs after the file-editing tool completes
An instruction added to CLAUDE.md asking Claude to format files after edits
A custom slash command that the developer types to format files
Enabling plan mode so Claude proposes formatting before acting
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the Claude Code harness executes deterministically on lifecycle events; a PostToolUse hook fires after a tool (such as an edit) runs, making formatting automatic and reliable. A CLAUDE.md instruction is only guidance the model may or may not follow, so it is not deterministic. A slash command must be manually invoked by the developer, so it does not run automatically. Plan mode makes Claude present a plan before editing and does not run formatters after edits.
referenceText: Claude Code — Hooks

stem: Your team is deciding whether to move a large set of non-urgent classification jobs to the Message Batches API. Which two statements accurately describe the Message Batches API? (Select 2.)
options: Requests are processed asynchronously, with results typically available within 24 hours
It provides a 50% cost discount compared to standard (synchronous) Messages API calls
Responses stream token-by-token over SSE as each individual request completes
It is the recommended choice for latency-sensitive, interactive chat experiences
Each batch is limited to a maximum of 10 requests
rationale: The Message Batches API processes requests asynchronously and returns results within a 24-hour target window, and it offers roughly a 50% cost reduction versus standard calls, making it ideal for high-volume, non-urgent workloads. It does not stream results token-by-token via SSE (that is the synchronous streaming API); it is explicitly not intended for latency-sensitive interactive use because results are not real-time; and a batch supports up to 100,000 requests, far more than 10.
referenceText: Message Batches API — batch processing overview

stem: A developer is building a customer-support chatbot on the Messages API. After the user's first message and Claude's reply, they append the next user turn and resend the full list. What is the correct structure of the `messages` array they must send on each request?
options: An alternating sequence of `user` and `assistant` turns, beginning with a `user` turn, containing the full prior conversation
Only the most recent `user` message, because the API stores prior turns server-side by session ID
An array that must begin with an `assistant` turn so Claude knows its prior context
A single concatenated string containing all turns separated by newline characters
rationale: The Messages API is stateless: the client must resend the entire conversation each request as an array of alternating `user`/`assistant` turns that begins with a `user` turn. The API does not persist conversation state server-side, so 'only the most recent message' is wrong. It must start with `user`, not `assistant`. `messages` is a structured array of role objects, not a concatenated string.
referenceText: Messages API — messages must be an alternating list of user/assistant turns; the API is stateless.

stem: When defining a tool for Claude, a developer specifies the parameters the tool accepts. In which field of the tool definition do they describe the accepted parameters, and what format does it use?
options: `input_schema`, expressed as a JSON Schema object
`parameters`, expressed as an OpenAPI path definition
`arguments`, expressed as a Python type-hint string
`input_schema`, expressed as a plain-text description of each field
rationale: A tool definition uses `input_schema`, a JSON Schema object describing the tool's expected input (types, properties, required fields). The field is not named `parameters` or `arguments`, and it is a structured JSON Schema object, not OpenAPI paths, Python hints, or free text. A separate `description` field holds natural-language guidance.
referenceText: Tool use — each tool has a name, description, and input_schema (JSON Schema).

stem: A developer wants to guarantee that Claude calls the `get_weather` tool on the next turn rather than replying in prose. Which `tool_choice` setting achieves this?
options: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}`
`{"type": "auto"}`
`{"type": "none"}`
`{"type": "required", "name": "get_weather"}`
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}` forces Claude to invoke that specific named tool. `auto` lets the model decide whether to use any tool. `none` prevents tool use entirely. `required` (i.e. `any`) forces *some* tool but does not take a `name` to pin one specific tool.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice supports auto, any, tool (named), and none.

stem: Claude returns a response with `stop_reason: "tool_use"` containing a `tool_use` block. The developer runs the tool and must send the output back. Which statements about returning the result are correct? (Select 2.)
options: The result is sent in a new `user`-role message containing a `tool_result` content block
The `tool_result` block must include a `tool_use_id` that matches the `id` of the corresponding `tool_use` block
The result must be sent in an `assistant`-role message so it continues Claude's turn
The prior `assistant` turn's `tool_use` block should be removed before sending the result
The `tool_result` must be sent as a top-level request field named `tool_output`, not inside `messages`
rationale: Tool results are returned in a `user`-role message as a `tool_result` content block whose `tool_use_id` matches the originating `tool_use` block's `id`. Results go in the `user` turn, not `assistant`. The assistant's `tool_use` block must be preserved in the conversation history so the ids line up. There is no top-level `tool_output` field.
referenceText: Tool use — return results in a user message with a tool_result block referencing tool_use_id.

stem: A developer enables streaming (`stream: true`) and wants to accumulate the incremental text as it arrives. Which server-sent event carries the incremental text fragments?
options: `content_block_delta` events, each with a `text_delta`
`message_start`, which contains the full assembled text
`content_block_stop`, which contains the completed text for that block
`ping` events, which carry buffered text between heartbeats
rationale: Streaming emits `content_block_delta` events carrying `text_delta` (or `input_json_delta`) fragments that the client concatenates. `message_start` contains the initial message shell with empty content. `content_block_stop` signals a block finished but carries no text payload. `ping` events are keep-alive heartbeats with no content.
referenceText: Streaming — content_block_delta events carry text_delta fragments.

stem: A team must classify 500,000 archived documents overnight where latency per item does not matter. They are evaluating the Message Batches API. Which statements are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: Batch processing offers a cost discount compared to standard synchronous requests
Batches are processed asynchronously, with a target completion window of 24 hours
Batch requests return results in a guaranteed real-time stream as each item finishes
Each request in a batch must omit a custom identifier so results can be matched by array order only
Batches guarantee that all requests complete within 5 seconds regardless of size
rationale: The Batches API processes large request sets asynchronously within a 24-hour target window at a reduced cost (50% off) versus synchronous calls. It is not real-time streaming. Each request carries a `custom_id` so results (which may return in any order) can be correlated; ids are required, not omitted. There is no 5-second guarantee.
referenceText: Message Batches — asynchronous processing, ~24h window, 50% cost discount, custom_id per request.

stem: A developer prepends a large, static system prompt (a long style guide) to thousands of requests and wants to reduce cost and latency for the repeated portion using prompt caching. How do they mark the reusable prefix?
options: Add a `cache_control` block of type `ephemeral` at the end of the content they want cached
Set a top-level request parameter `cache: true` to cache the entire prompt automatically
Pass a `cache_id` returned from a prior request to reuse the stored prompt
Wrap the reusable text in `<cache>` XML tags inside the system prompt
rationale: Prompt caching is enabled by placing a `cache_control` breakpoint (type `ephemeral`) on the content block marking the end of the cacheable prefix; everything up to that breakpoint is cached. There is no `cache: true` flag, no returned `cache_id` to pass back, and no `<cache>` XML tag convention.
referenceText: Prompt caching — mark the cacheable prefix with a cache_control ephemeral breakpoint.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking and sets `budget_tokens` to 10,000. What must be true about the relationship between `budget_tokens` and `max_tokens`?
options: `budget_tokens` must be less than `max_tokens`, since thinking tokens count toward the overall output budget
`budget_tokens` must be greater than `max_tokens` to reserve room for reasoning
`budget_tokens` and `max_tokens` must be exactly equal
`max_tokens` is ignored entirely whenever extended thinking is enabled
rationale: Thinking tokens are part of the model's output, so `budget_tokens` must be strictly less than `max_tokens`, leaving room for the visible response. It cannot exceed or equal `max_tokens` in a way that leaves no room for the answer, and `max_tokens` still governs total output.
referenceText: Extended thinking — budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens.

stem: A developer is running an agentic tool-use loop with extended thinking enabled. When they pass tool results back for the next turn, what must they do with the `thinking` blocks from the prior assistant turn?
options: Preserve and pass the prior `thinking` blocks back unmodified along with the tool results
Strip all `thinking` blocks before resending, since they are internal-only
Convert each `thinking` block into a `user` message summarizing the reasoning
Replace the `thinking` blocks with an empty string to save tokens
rationale: During a tool-use loop with extended thinking, the previous turn's `thinking` (and `redacted_thinking`) blocks must be passed back unmodified so the model can maintain reasoning continuity and signature verification. Stripping, converting, or blanking them breaks the loop and can cause errors.
referenceText: Extended thinking with tool use — preserve thinking blocks across turns.

stem: A product team needs the fastest, most cost-efficient Claude model for a high-volume, latency-sensitive classification endpoint where deep multi-step reasoning is not required. Consulting the models overview, which family is the best fit?
options: A Claude Haiku model, optimized for speed and cost
A Claude Opus model, optimized for maximum reasoning depth
Whichever model has the largest maximum output token limit, regardless of speed
Any model, since all Claude models have identical latency and price
rationale: Haiku models are positioned as the fastest and most cost-effective tier, ideal for high-volume, latency-sensitive work. Opus targets the hardest reasoning tasks at higher cost/latency. Output token limit is not the right selection axis here, and the models differ meaningfully in speed and price.
referenceText: Models overview — Haiku for speed/cost, Opus for deepest reasoning.

stem: A developer wants to build a custom coding agent programmatically that reuses Claude Code's harness (context gathering, tool execution, permissions) rather than orchestrating raw Messages API calls by hand. Which Anthropic offering is designed for this?
options: The Claude Agent SDK
The Message Batches API
The Text Completions (legacy) API
The Prompt Caching endpoint
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the same agent harness that powers Claude Code (agentic loop, tool use, file and command execution, permissions, MCP) for building custom agents. Batches is for bulk async inference, legacy Text Completions is a superseded raw-completion API, and prompt caching is a cost feature, not an agent framework.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK — build agents on the same harness as Claude Code.

stem: A team standardizes their repository for Claude Code by adding a `CLAUDE.md` file. Which statements about `CLAUDE.md` are correct? (Select 2.)
options: It is automatically pulled into context at the start of a session to give Claude project-specific guidance
It is a good place to record build/test commands, code style, and repository conventions
It must be written in JSON so Claude Code can parse it deterministically
It only takes effect if the user manually pastes its contents into every prompt
It disables all of Claude Code's built-in tools until the file is deleted
rationale: `CLAUDE.md` is a memory file loaded automatically into context, ideal for build/test commands, style rules, and conventions. It is Markdown (prose), not required JSON. It loads automatically rather than needing manual pasting, and it does not disable any tools.
referenceText: Claude Code memory — CLAUDE.md auto-loads project guidance and conventions.

stem: A team wants a reusable `/deploy-check` command in Claude Code that always runs the same prompt template. How do they create a custom project slash command?
options: Add a Markdown file named `deploy-check.md` under the project's `.claude/commands/` directory
Register the command in `package.json` under a `claudeCommands` key
Define it as an environment variable `CLAUDE_SLASH_DEPLOY_CHECK`
Custom slash commands are not supported; only built-in commands exist
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files placed in `.claude/commands/` (project) or `~/.claude/commands/` (personal); the filename becomes the command name. They are not registered in `package.json`, not defined via environment variables, and custom commands are fully supported.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands — Markdown files in .claude/commands/.

stem: A team wants Claude Code to automatically run a formatter every time it writes or edits a file, without relying on Claude to remember. Which Claude Code feature enforces this deterministically?
options: A hook configured in settings that fires on the tool event (e.g. PostToolUse)
A line in CLAUDE.md politely asking Claude to always format after editing
Plan mode, which formats files as part of presenting the plan
A custom slash command the user must remember to type after each edit
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness runs deterministically on lifecycle/tool events (such as PostToolUse), guaranteeing the formatter runs regardless of the model's behavior. A CLAUDE.md request is advisory and can be skipped. Plan mode does not format files, and a manual slash command still depends on the user remembering.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks — deterministic shell commands on tool/lifecycle events.

stem: A developer asks Claude Code to explore a large refactor but wants it to research and propose an approach without editing any files until approved. Which Claude Code capability is designed for this?
options: Plan mode, which lets Claude analyze and propose steps without making changes until approved
Auto-accept mode, which applies all edits immediately
Batch mode, which queues all edits for a 24-hour window
Streaming mode, which shows edits token by token as they are written
rationale: Plan mode restricts Claude to read-only analysis so it can research and present a plan before any files are modified, with changes applied only after the user approves. Auto-accept does the opposite. There is no edit-queuing 'batch mode' in Claude Code, and streaming concerns output rendering, not a read-only guarantee.
referenceText: Claude Code — plan mode for read-only analysis before changes.

stem: A developer is implementing an MCP server and wants to understand the three core primitives it can expose. Which of the following are MCP primitives? (Select 3.)
options: Tools — functions the model can invoke to take actions
Resources — data/content the server exposes for context (e.g. files, records)
Prompts — reusable prompt templates the server offers to clients
Weights — model checkpoints the server streams to the client
Schedulers — cron jobs the client registers on the server
rationale: MCP servers expose three primitives: tools (invocable functions/actions), resources (contextual data the client can read), and prompts (reusable templated messages). 'Weights' and 'schedulers' are not MCP concepts; MCP is a protocol for connecting models to context and tools, not for distributing model checkpoints or cron jobs.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol — tools, resources, and prompts primitives.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, a developer wants their server to expose read-only reference data (such as a document or database row) that the client can pull into the model's context. Which MCP primitive fits, and what is its control model?
options: Resources — typically application- or user-controlled, providing context data rather than performing actions
Tools — always model-controlled and invoked to perform side-effecting actions
Prompts — model-controlled templates that the server executes automatically
Sampling — a resource type the server uses to call the client's model
rationale: Resources expose read-only context data and are generally application/user-controlled (the host decides what to surface). Tools are the model-controlled, action-performing primitive. Prompts are user-controlled templates, not auto-executed. Sampling lets a server request a completion from the client, but it is not a resource.
referenceText: MCP resources — context data, application/user-controlled.

stem: An MCP server author wants to offer users reusable, parameterized message templates (e.g. a 'summarize-git-diff' template) that appear as selectable options in the client UI. Which MCP primitive should they implement?
options: Prompts
Resources
Tools
Roots
rationale: MCP prompts are user-controlled, reusable templates (often parameterized) that clients can surface as selectable commands. Resources expose context data, tools perform model-invoked actions, and 'roots' define filesystem boundaries the client grants a server — none of these are the templating primitive.
referenceText: MCP prompts — reusable, user-controlled templates.

stem: A developer's application sets `max_tokens: 256` and receives a response with `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`. What does this indicate and what is the appropriate handling?
options: The output was truncated at the token limit; increase `max_tokens` or continue the response if the full output is needed
The model refused the request on safety grounds; rephrase the prompt
The model finished naturally at a stop point; no action is needed
A tool call is pending; run the tool and return a tool_result
rationale: `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` means generation was cut off because it hit the `max_tokens` cap, so the output may be incomplete; the fix is to raise the limit or continue. `end_turn` (not shown) is the natural-completion reason, `tool_use` signals a pending tool call, and there is no safety-refusal implied by this stop reason.
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason values including max_tokens, end_turn, tool_use.

stem: A developer wants to give Claude a consistent persona and behavioral instructions that apply across every turn of a Messages API conversation. Where should these instructions go?
options: In the top-level `system` parameter of the request
As the first entry in the `messages` array with role `system`
Appended to every `assistant` turn so the model re-reads them
In the `metadata` field, which the model treats as instructions
rationale: System-level instructions and persona belong in the dedicated top-level `system` parameter. The Messages API does not use a `system` role inside the `messages` array (only `user`/`assistant`). Persona should not be stuffed into assistant turns, and `metadata` is for request tagging (e.g. user_id), not model instructions.
referenceText: Messages API — the system parameter sets system-level instructions.

stem: A developer sends an image plus a question to Claude in a single user turn. How is multimodal content represented in the Messages API?
options: As a `content` array on the user message containing both an `image` block and a `text` block
As two separate requests, one with the image and one with the text, correlated by request id
As a top-level `image_url` request parameter alongside the `messages` array
As a base64 string concatenated directly into the text of the message
rationale: A message's `content` can be an array of typed blocks; an image is an `image` block (base64 or URL source) placed alongside a `text` block in the same user turn. It is not split into separate correlated requests, there is no top-level `image_url` parameter, and image data must be a structured `image` block, not raw text concatenation.
referenceText: Messages API / vision — content array with image and text blocks.

stem: During a streaming response, a developer needs the final token usage and the reason generation stopped. Which streaming event carries the final `stop_reason` and usage deltas?
options: `message_delta`, which contains top-level changes like stop_reason and output token usage
`content_block_start`, emitted once per block with final usage
`ping`, which periodically reports cumulative usage
`message_start`, which already contains the final stop_reason
rationale: The `message_delta` event carries top-level updates to the message object, including the final `stop_reason`, `stop_sequence`, and cumulative output `usage`. `content_block_start` opens a content block, `ping` is a heartbeat, and `message_start` is emitted before generation completes so it cannot hold the final stop_reason.
referenceText: Streaming — message_delta carries final stop_reason and usage.

stem: A developer defines two tools and, after Claude returns two `tool_use` blocks in a single assistant turn, wants to reply correctly. How should the tool results be returned?
options: In one `user` message containing two `tool_result` blocks, each referencing its matching `tool_use_id`
In two separate `user` messages, one per result, sent as consecutive turns without an assistant turn between them
In a single `assistant` message combining both results into one `tool_result` block
By resending the request with `tool_choice` set to none so Claude answers directly
rationale: When an assistant turn contains multiple `tool_use` blocks, all results are returned together in a single following `user` message with one `tool_result` block per call, each matched by `tool_use_id`. Splitting into consecutive user turns breaks alternation, results never go in an `assistant` message, and forcing `tool_choice: none` abandons the pending calls.
referenceText: Tool use — return parallel tool results as multiple tool_result blocks in one user message.

stem: A tool executed by the developer's backend fails (e.g. the weather API times out). How should this be communicated back to Claude so it can react gracefully?
options: Return a `tool_result` block with `is_error: true` and an error description in the content
Omit the `tool_result` entirely so Claude retries automatically
Send the error as a new `system` prompt describing the failure
Set the top-level request field `error: true` for that turn
rationale: Tool execution failures are reported by returning the `tool_result` block with `is_error: true` and an explanatory message, letting Claude adjust (retry, apologize, try an alternative). Omitting the result leaves a dangling tool_use and errors the request; there is no per-turn `system` re-injection pattern for this, and no top-level `error` field.
referenceText: Tool use — signal failures with is_error on the tool_result block.

stem: You are calling the Messages API and want to give Claude a persistent instruction like "You are a terse SQL assistant that only returns valid PostgreSQL." Where should this instruction be placed in the request?
options: As the top-level `system` parameter of the request
As the first element of the `messages` array with `role: "system"`
As a `system` content block inside the first user message
As metadata under the `metadata.system` field
rationale: The Messages API takes system instructions via the dedicated top-level `system` parameter, not as a message role. Distractor 1 is wrong because the API only accepts `user` and `assistant` roles in `messages`; there is no `system` role. Distractor 2 invents a non-existent content block, and distractor 3 references a `metadata` field that only carries things like `user_id`, not system prompts.
referenceText: Messages API — system prompts are supplied via the top-level `system` parameter; message roles are limited to user and assistant.

stem: When defining a tool for Claude to use, which field describes the tool's expected parameters and must be a valid JSON Schema object?
options: `input_schema`
`parameters`
`arguments`
`function_schema`
rationale: Each tool definition in the Anthropic API uses `name`, `description`, and `input_schema`, where `input_schema` is a JSON Schema object describing the inputs. `parameters` and `function_schema` are not the field names used by the Messages API (the first resembles OpenAI's convention), and `arguments` is not a tool-definition field either.
referenceText: Tool use overview — tools are defined with name, description, and an input_schema JSON Schema object.

stem: Your application must guarantee that, on a particular request, Claude calls the `get_weather` tool and nothing else. Which `tool_choice` value accomplishes this?
options: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}`
`{"type": "any"}`
`{"type": "auto", "name": "get_weather"}`
`{"type": "required", "tool": "get_weather"}`
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}` forces Claude to invoke that specific named tool. `{"type": "any"}` forces the model to use one of the available tools but does not restrict which one. `auto` (the default when tools are present) lets the model decide whether to use a tool and ignores a `name` field. `required` with a `tool` key is not a valid Anthropic `tool_choice` shape.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice supports auto, any, tool (with name), and none.

stem: Claude has responded with a `tool_use` content block for your `get_weather` tool. After your code executes the function, how do you return the output so Claude can continue?
options: Send a new `user` message containing a `tool_result` block whose `tool_use_id` matches the `tool_use` block's `id`
Send a new `assistant` message containing a `tool_result` block with the raw output
Append the output to the `system` prompt and resend the conversation
Call a separate `/v1/tools/submit` endpoint with the tool output
rationale: Tool results are returned as a `tool_result` content block inside a new `user`-role message, and its `tool_use_id` must reference the `id` of the corresponding `tool_use` block. Tool results are not sent as an `assistant` message, are not injected into the system prompt, and there is no separate tool-submission endpoint—everything flows through the Messages API.
referenceText: Tool use — return results in a user message with a tool_result block referencing tool_use_id.

stem: Your client is consuming a streaming (SSE) response from the Messages API with `stream: true`. Which statements about the stream are correct? (Select 2.)
options: A stream begins with a `message_start` event and ends with a `message_stop` event
Incremental text arrives in `content_block_delta` events
The full assembled message is delivered once in a single `message_complete` event
Each token is delivered as its own top-level `token` event outside of any content block
You must poll a status endpoint between events to advance the stream
rationale: Anthropic streaming uses server-sent events beginning with `message_start` and ending with `message_stop`, with text arriving via `content_block_delta` (text_delta) events. There is no single `message_complete` payload—the client assembles the message from deltas. Tokens are delivered inside content-block deltas, not as standalone `token` events, and SSE is push-based, so no polling between events is required.
referenceText: Streaming — SSE event sequence: message_start, content_block_start/delta/stop, message_delta, message_stop.

stem: You need to process 40,000 non-urgent document-summarization requests overnight at lower cost. Which Anthropic capability is the best fit?
options: The Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously at a discount
The streaming Messages API with `stream: true`
Prompt caching with a 1-hour cache TTL
Extended thinking with a large `budget_tokens`
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large volumes of asynchronous, non-time-sensitive requests and offers a 50% cost discount, making it ideal here. Streaming only changes how a single synchronous response is delivered. Prompt caching reduces cost only when large prompt prefixes are reused, and extended thinking increases—not decreases—cost and latency.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous bulk processing at a 50% discount.

stem: In the Message Batches API, what is the purpose of the `custom_id` you assign to each request in a batch?
options: To correlate each result back to the original request when results return out of order
To set the priority in which requests within the batch are processed
To authenticate the individual request against your API key
To specify which model each request should use
rationale: Batch results are not guaranteed to return in submission order, so each request carries a `custom_id` that you use to match a result to its originating request. It has nothing to do with prioritization, authentication (handled by the API key/header), or model selection (set per-request in the `params`).
referenceText: Message Batches API — each request includes a custom_id used to match results back to inputs.

stem: Which mechanism enables prompt caching for a large, reused instruction block in a Messages API request?
options: Adding a `cache_control` field with `{"type": "ephemeral"}` to the content block
Setting a top-level `cache: true` request parameter
Passing a `cache_id` returned from a previous response
Enabling caching once in the Console; no request changes are needed
rationale: Prompt caching is opt-in per content block by attaching `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}`, which marks a cache breakpoint covering everything up to that point.
