There is no top-level `cache` boolean, no `cache_id` handoff between requests, and caching is controlled in the request body rather than a Console toggle.
referenceText: Prompt caching — mark cacheable prefixes with cache_control type ephemeral.

stem: A developer sets `max_tokens: 4096` and wants to enable extended thinking. Which configuration is valid?
options: Set `thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 2048}` so the budget is less than max_tokens
Set `thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 4096}` so the budget equals max_tokens
Set `thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 8192}` to exceed max_tokens for deeper reasoning
Set `extended_thinking: true` as a top-level boolean parameter
rationale: When extended thinking is enabled, `budget_tokens` must be less than `max_tokens`, because the thinking budget is drawn from the overall output allowance. A budget equal to or greater than max_tokens leaves no room for the final answer and is invalid. There is also no top-level `extended_thinking` boolean; thinking is configured via the `thinking` object with a `budget_tokens` value.
referenceText: Extended thinking — configure via the thinking object; budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens.

stem: You are building a high-volume, latency-sensitive ticket-classification service where each request is short and simple. Which Claude model tier is generally the most appropriate starting choice?
options: A Haiku model, optimized for speed and cost efficiency
An Opus model, optimized for the most complex reasoning
Whichever model has the largest context window regardless of tier
A Sonnet model is the only tier that supports classification
rationale: Haiku models are the fastest and most cost-effective tier, which suits high-volume, simple, latency-sensitive tasks like short-text classification. Opus targets the hardest reasoning at higher cost/latency, context-window size is not the deciding factor for simple classification, and classification is not exclusive to Sonnet—every tier can classify.
referenceText: Models overview — Haiku is the fastest/most cost-effective tier; Opus the most capable; Sonnet balanced.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol (MCP), primitives differ by who controls their invocation. Which statements correctly describe the control model? (Select 2.)
options: Tools are model-controlled: the LLM decides when to invoke them
Prompts are user-controlled: typically surfaced for a user to select and trigger
Resources are model-controlled: the LLM autonomously writes to them each turn
Tools are user-controlled: only the end user may invoke them
Prompts are server-controlled: the server invokes them without user or model input
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled (the model chooses to call them), prompts are user-controlled (exposed as commands/templates the user selects), and resources are application-controlled contextual data. Option 3 mischaracterizes resources as model-written; option 4 contradicts the model-controlled nature of tools; option 5 wrongly labels prompts as server-invoked.
referenceText: MCP concepts — tools are model-controlled, resources application-controlled, prompts user-controlled.

stem: An MCP server wants to expose read-only reference data (like file contents or database records) that the host application can load into context, analogous to a GET request with no side effects. Which MCP primitive fits?
options: Resources
Tools
Prompts
Sampling
rationale: Resources represent contextual, typically read-only data identified by URIs that the host application supplies to the model—conceptually like a GET with no side effects. Tools are for executing actions (often with side effects), prompts are reusable templated interactions, and sampling is the server-initiated request for the client to run an LLM completion, not a data-exposure primitive.
referenceText: MCP resources — expose read-only contextual data (GET-like, no side effects) to the host.

stem: A team wants their MCP server to offer reusable, parameterized message templates (for example, a "summarize-git-diff" workflow) that users can invoke on demand, often as slash commands in the client UI. Which MCP primitive should they implement?
options: Prompts
Tools
Resources
Roots
rationale: MCP prompts are user-controlled, reusable templates (which can accept arguments) that clients commonly surface as slash commands or menu items for the user to invoke. Tools are model-invoked action functions, resources are contextual data, and roots define filesystem/URI boundaries the server may operate within—none of which are templated user-invoked messages.
referenceText: MCP prompts — reusable, user-invoked templated messages, often surfaced as slash commands.

stem: What is the primary purpose of a `CLAUDE.md` file in a Claude Code project?
options: To provide persistent project context (conventions, commands, architecture) that Claude Code automatically loads into memory
To store the API key and model configuration for the session
To define a CI pipeline that runs whenever Claude edits a file
To list which files Claude Code is forbidden from reading
rationale: `CLAUDE.md` is a memory file automatically pulled into context so Claude Code knows project conventions, key commands, and architecture. It is not a secrets/config store (API keys live in environment/settings, not committed memory), it is not a CI definition, and it is not an access-control denylist.
referenceText: Claude Code memory — CLAUDE.md holds project context loaded automatically into the session.

stem: You create a custom Claude Code slash command as a Markdown file in `.claude/commands/`. How do you make the command incorporate text the user types after the command name?
options: Reference the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command's Markdown body
Wrap the command body in a `<user_input>` XML tag
Add a `params:` list to YAML frontmatter and read `${{ params.text }}`
User text is passed automatically as the first line; no placeholder is needed
rationale: Custom slash commands substitute the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder with whatever the user types after the command, letting one command template accept dynamic input. There is no special `<user_input>` tag convention, no `params`/`${{ }}` templating syntax for basic argument passing, and arguments are not auto-prepended—you must reference the placeholder.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands — use the $ARGUMENTS placeholder to inject user-provided text.

stem: A team wants to automatically run a formatter every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, without relying on Claude to remember to do it. Which Claude Code feature enforces this?
options: A PostToolUse hook configured in settings.json
A note in CLAUDE.md asking Claude to format after edits
A custom slash command the developer runs manually
Plan mode, which formats files during planning
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness runs deterministically at lifecycle events; a PostToolUse hook fires after a tool like Edit/Write, making it the reliable way to auto-format. A CLAUDE.md note is only a suggestion the model may or may not follow, a manual slash command requires remembering to invoke it, and plan mode is for read-only planning and does not modify files.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks — shell commands run at events like PreToolUse/PostToolUse for deterministic automation.

stem: What is the defining behavior of Claude Code's plan mode?
options: Claude researches the codebase and proposes a plan without making any file edits until you approve
Claude executes all planned edits immediately and shows the diff afterward
Claude disables tool use entirely and only chats
Claude commits changes to a new branch automatically
rationale: Plan mode restricts Claude Code to read-only investigation so it can present a plan for approval before touching files, giving you a checkpoint before execution. It does not auto-execute edits, it still uses read/search tools (so it is not a chat-only mode), and it does not create branches or commit on its own.
referenceText: Claude Code plan mode — read-only research and planning before making edits.

stem: In a tool-use flow, your Messages API response comes back with `stop_reason: "tool_use"`. What does this signal to your application?
options: Claude wants you to execute one or more tools and return the results before it continues
Claude has finished and no further action is needed
An error occurred while Claude tried to run the tool internally
Claude reached `max_tokens` before completing the tool call
rationale: `stop_reason: "tool_use"` means the response contains one or more `tool_use` blocks and Claude is pausing for your code to run those tools and return `tool_result` blocks. It is not a terminal completion (that would be `end_turn`), it is not an error, and hitting the token cap would surface as `max_tokens`. Note the model never executes tools itself—your application does.
referenceText: Tool use — a response with stop_reason tool_use requires the client to run tools and return results.

stem: The Messages API is stateless. To continue a multi-turn conversation, what must your application do on each new request?
options: Resend the full prior conversation history in the `messages` array plus the new user turn
Pass the `conversation_id` returned by the previous response
Rely on the server retaining prior turns tied to your API key
Include only the latest user message; the model recalls earlier context automatically
rationale: Because the API keeps no server-side session, you must include the accumulated history (previous user and assistant messages) along with the new turn on every request. There is no `conversation_id` handle, the server does not persist prior turns for your key, and sending only the latest message discards all earlier context.
referenceText: Messages API — stateless; the client sends prior turns to maintain conversation context.

stem: While streaming a tool call, how are the tool's input arguments delivered incrementally in the SSE stream?
options: As `input_json_delta` fragments within `content_block_delta` events that you concatenate into the JSON input
As a single fully-formed `tool_input` event once the arguments are complete
As `text_delta` events that you parse as plain prose
Tool inputs are never streamed and only appear in a separate non-streamed response
rationale: During streaming, a tool_use block's arguments arrive as `input_json_delta` fragments inside `content_block_delta` events; the client accumulates the partial JSON strings and parses them once the block completes. They do not come as one atomic `tool_input` event, they are distinct from `text_delta` (which carries prose), and tool inputs are indeed streamable within the same SSE response.
referenceText: Streaming — tool inputs stream as partial JSON via input_json_delta within content_block_delta.

stem: Which practices help maximize prompt-caching effectiveness in the Messages API? (Select 2.)
options: Place stable, reused content (system instructions, large context) before any variable content so the cached prefix stays identical
Ensure the cached prefix meets the model's minimum cacheable token threshold
Put the frequently-changing user question at the very top of the prompt
Attach `cache_control` to every small message regardless of size for maximum coverage
Regenerate a new cache breakpoint on every request to force fresh writes
rationale: Caching only helps when a byte-identical prefix is reused, so stable content belongs before variable content, and the prefix must exceed the model's minimum cacheable token count (e.g., ~1024 tokens for larger models) to be eligible. Putting the changing question first invalidates the shared prefix, caching tiny sub-minimum blocks yields no cache hit, and deliberately forcing fresh writes every request defeats the purpose (you want cache reads).
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache stable prefixes above the minimum token threshold; order static content first.

stem: You are building a chat feature on the Messages API. Before the model has generated anything, your backend needs to supply the persona and formatting rules that should govern every turn of the conversation. Where should these instructions be placed in the request?
options: As the top-level `system` parameter
As the first element of the `messages` array with role `system`
As a `user` message prepended before the real user turn
As metadata inside the final `assistant` message
rationale: The Messages API accepts a top-level `system` string parameter for system-level instructions; it is not a message role. There is no `system` role inside the `messages` array, so option 1 would be rejected. Embedding the rules in a user message (option 2) works poorly and mixes instructions with user content, and there is no assistant metadata field for this (option 3).
referenceText: Messages API — system prompts are provided via the top-level `system` parameter, not as a message role.

stem: Your application sends a conversation to the Messages API but receives a 400 error. The `messages` array is: [user, user, assistant]. What is the most likely cause?
options: Messages must alternate between `user` and `assistant` roles and begin with a `user` turn
The array must always end with a `user` message
Consecutive `user` messages are allowed only if a `system` prompt is present
Each message must include a `tool_use_id` field
rationale: The Messages API requires roles to alternate and the first message to be `user`; two consecutive `user` turns violate the alternation rule and trigger a 400. The array typically ends with the message you want the model to respond to, but the binding rule that is broken here is alternation (option 0). Consecutive user turns are not conditionally allowed by a system prompt (option 2), and `tool_use_id` only appears in tool_result blocks (option 3).
referenceText: Messages API — conversational turns must alternate user/assistant and start with user.

stem: You define a tool for the model and want to guarantee that on this particular request the model calls that exact tool rather than replying with text. Which `tool_choice` setting achieves this?
options: `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}`
`{"type": "auto"}`
`{"type": "any"}`
`{"type": "none"}`
rationale: `{"type": "tool", "name": ...}` forces the model to invoke that specific named tool. `auto` lets the model decide whether to use a tool at all; `any` forces some tool but not a specific one; `none` prevents tool use entirely.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice type `tool` forces a specific named tool.

stem: Inside a tool definition, what is the purpose of the `input_schema` field?
options: It is a JSON Schema that defines the structure and types of the parameters the model must produce to call the tool
It contains the tool's return value schema that the API validates responses against
It is a natural-language description the model reads to decide when to use the tool
It maps the tool to a specific model version that supports it
rationale: `input_schema` is a JSON Schema object describing the tool's input parameters (names, types, which are required); the model uses it to generate a valid tool_use input. It does not describe the return value (option 1) — that is conveyed via tool_result. The natural-language guidance the model reads to decide usage is the `description` field, not input_schema (option 2). It has no role in model version mapping (option 3).
referenceText: Tool use — input_schema is a JSON Schema describing tool input parameters.

stem: The model returns a response with `stop_reason: "tool_use"` containing a tool_use block. Your code executes the function. How must you return the result to continue the conversation?
options: Append a new `user` message containing a `tool_result` block whose `tool_use_id` matches the tool_use block's `id`
Append a new `assistant` message containing a `tool_result` block
Send the result in the top-level `tool_outputs` parameter of the next request
Modify the original `assistant` message to include the function's return value
rationale: Tool results are returned as a `tool_result` content block inside a new `user` message, and the block's `tool_use_id` must match the `id` of the corresponding tool_use block. tool_result blocks are not placed in assistant messages (option 1), there is no top-level `tool_outputs` parameter (option 2), and you never edit the prior assistant turn (option 3).
referenceText: Tool use — return tool_result in a user message with matching tool_use_id.

stem: A tool your app runs fails (e.g., the external API times out). How should you communicate this back to the model so it can react appropriately?
options: Return a `tool_result` block with `is_error: true` and an error description in its content
Omit the tool_result block so the model retries automatically
Return a top-level HTTP 500 from your request to the Messages API
Set `stop_reason: "error"` in the next request
rationale: A tool_result block supports `is_error: true`, letting you report a failure in content that the model can read and adapt to. Omitting the block (option 1) breaks the required pairing and causes an API error. You control your request, not the API's HTTP status (option 2), and `stop_reason` is a response field set by the model, not a request field (option 3).
referenceText: Tool use — tool_result supports is_error to signal tool failures.

stem: You enable streaming (`stream: true`) on a Messages request. Which server-sent event carries the incremental text tokens as they are generated?
options: `content_block_delta` events with a `text_delta`
`message_start` events
`content_block_start` events
`message_stop` events
rationale: Incremental output arrives in `content_block_delta` events whose delta is a `text_delta` (or `input_json_delta` for tool inputs, `thinking_delta` for thinking). `message_start` carries the initial message shell, `content_block_start` marks the beginning of a block, and `message_stop` signals the end of the stream — none of them carry the token text.
referenceText: Streaming — text tokens arrive in content_block_delta text_delta events.

stem: When consuming a streaming Messages response, which events mark the overall lifecycle boundaries of the message itself (as opposed to individual content blocks)? (Select 2.)
options: `message_start`
`message_stop`
`content_block_start`
`content_block_delta`
`ping`
rationale: `message_start` opens the stream with the initial message object and `message_stop` closes it — these bound the whole message. `content_block_start` and `content_block_delta` operate at the individual content-block level, and `ping` events are periodic keep-alives with no lifecycle meaning.
referenceText: Streaming — message_start and message_stop bound the message lifecycle.

stem: Your nightly job classifies 40,000 documents where results are not needed for several hours. You want the lowest cost per request. Which approach best fits?
options: Submit the requests through the Message Batches API
Send 40,000 concurrent streaming Messages requests
Use a single Messages request with all documents concatenated
Enable extended thinking on each Messages request
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume, latency-tolerant asynchronous workloads and provides a significant cost discount versus standard requests, making it ideal here. Firing tens of thousands of concurrent requests (option 1) risks rate limits and offers no discount; concatenating everything into one request (option 2) blows past context limits and mixes documents; extended thinking (option 3) raises cost and latency, the opposite of the goal.
referenceText: Message Batches — asynchronous, high-volume processing at a discount.

stem: When constructing a Message Batches request, what is the role of the `custom_id` field on each entry?
options: It is a caller-supplied identifier used to match each result back to its original request
It selects which model processes that individual request
It sets the priority order in which requests are processed
It is the batch-level identifier returned to poll for completion
rationale: Each request in a batch carries a `custom_id` you supply; because results may return in any order, you use it to correlate each result to its input. It does not choose a model (option 1) or set priority (option 2). The identifier used to poll the batch's status is the batch `id` returned at creation, which is distinct from per-request custom_id (option 3).
referenceText: Message Batches — custom_id correlates results to requests.

stem: You send a chatbot a large, unchanging system prompt and knowledge base on every turn. To reduce cost and latency on repeated calls, you add prompt caching. Which mechanism marks the reusable prefix?
options: A `cache_control` block with `type: "ephemeral"` placed at the end of the content you want cached
A top-level `cache: true` request parameter
An HTTP `Cache-Control` request header
Setting `tool_choice` to `cache`
rationale: Prompt caching is enabled by adding a `cache_control` breakpoint (currently `type: "ephemeral"`) to the content block marking the end of the cacheable prefix. There is no top-level `cache` parameter (option 1), the feature is not driven by the HTTP Cache-Control header (option 2), and `tool_choice` is unrelated to caching (option 3).
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache_control ephemeral breakpoints define cached prefixes.

stem: After enabling prompt caching, which usage field in the response tells you how many input tokens were served from an existing cache (rather than re-processed)?
options: `cache_read_input_tokens`
`cache_creation_input_tokens`
`input_tokens`
`output_tokens`
rationale: `cache_read_input_tokens` reports tokens served from a cache hit (billed at a reduced rate). `cache_creation_input_tokens` counts tokens written to the cache on a miss, `input_tokens` counts uncached input, and `output_tokens` counts generated tokens.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache_read_input_tokens reports cache hits.

stem: You enable extended thinking. Which constraint must your request satisfy for the `thinking.budget_tokens` value?
options: It must be less than `max_tokens`, since thinking tokens are drawn from the response budget
It must be greater than `max_tokens` to reserve extra space
It must exactly equal `max_tokens`
It has no relationship to `max_tokens` and can be any value
rationale: The thinking budget is part of the output token allowance, so `budget_tokens` must be set less than `max_tokens` to leave room for the final answer. Values greater than or equal to max_tokens (options 1 and 2) would be invalid or leave no room for the visible response, and the two are definitely related (option 3).
referenceText: Extended thinking — budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens.

stem: You use extended thinking together with tool use across multiple turns. What must you do with the `thinking` content blocks the model returns?
options: Pass them back unmodified in the subsequent request so the reasoning context is preserved
Strip them out before sending the next request to save tokens
Convert them into `user` messages before resending
Summarize them into the system prompt
rationale: When continuing a tool-use interaction with extended thinking, the previous assistant turn's thinking blocks (including any signature) must be passed back unaltered so the model retains its reasoning context. Stripping, converting, or summarizing them (options 1-3) breaks the continuity extended thinking relies on and can invalidate the request.
referenceText: Extended thinking — preserve thinking blocks across tool-use turns.

stem: A response comes back with `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`. What does this indicate?
options: The model hit the `max_tokens` limit and the output was truncated before a natural stopping point
The model finished its answer normally within the token budget
The model chose to call a tool
The model matched a configured stop sequence
rationale: `max_tokens` as a stop_reason means generation was cut off because it reached the output token cap, so the response may be incomplete. A normal completion is `end_turn` (option 1), a tool call is `tool_use` (option 2), and a stop-sequence match is `stop_sequence` (option 3).
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason max_tokens indicates truncation at the limit.

stem: You need to send an image plus a question in a single Messages request. How is the image supplied within a user message?
options: As an `image` content block (base64 source or URL) alongside a `text` block in the message's `content` array
As a top-level `image_url` request parameter
As a separate message with role `image`
By encoding the image bytes directly into the `system` prompt
rationale: Vision requests place an `image` content block (with a base64 or URL source) in the same message's `content` array as the accompanying `text` block. There is no top-level image parameter (option 1), no `image` message role (option 2), and images are not embedded in the system prompt (option 3).
referenceText: Vision — images are provided as image content blocks in message content.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, an MCP server exposes capabilities. Which primitive is model-controlled — meaning the model decides when to invoke it during a task?
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Sampling configuration
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled: the model can choose to call them to take actions or fetch data. Resources are application-controlled context that the host application decides how to surface, and prompts are user-controlled templates typically triggered by explicit user selection.
referenceText: MCP — tools are model-controlled primitives.

stem: An MCP server wants to expose read-only reference data (like files or database records) that the host application can pull into context. Which MCP primitive fits this use case?
options: Resources, identified by URIs
Tools, invoked via tools/call
Prompts, selected by the user
Roots, defined by the client
rationale: MCP resources represent data (identified by URIs) that the client/host can read and include as context — a good fit for read-only reference data. Tools are for model-invoked actions (option 1), prompts are user-triggered templates (option 2), and roots define filesystem boundaries the client shares with the server rather than exposing content (option 3).
referenceText: MCP — resources expose readable data via URIs.

stem: You want to give Claude Code persistent, project-specific context (build commands, conventions) that loads automatically every session in a repository. What is the standard mechanism?
options: A `CLAUDE.md` file committed at the project root
A `.env` file with a `CONTEXT` variable
A one-time message pasted at the start of each session
A `README.md` file, which Claude Code parses as memory
rationale: Claude Code automatically loads `CLAUDE.md` memory files (project root and user-level) into context at session start, making them the standard place for project conventions and commands. A `.env` variable is not read as memory (option 1), pasting context manually is not persistent (option 2), and while a README is useful documentation, CLAUDE.md — not README.md — is the dedicated memory file (option 3).
referenceText: Claude Code — CLAUDE.md memory files load project context automatically.

stem: You want a reusable Claude Code workflow that a teammate can invoke by typing `/deploy-check` and that accepts arguments. How do you define it?
options: Create a Markdown file in `.claude/commands/` whose name is the command and that can reference `$ARGUMENTS`
Register the command in `settings.json` under a `commands` key
Add a shell alias named `deploy-check` to your profile
Define it as a hook on the `UserPromptSubmit` event
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files placed in `.claude/commands/` (project) or the user commands directory; the filename becomes the command and `$ARGUMENTS` injects passed arguments. They are not registered in settings.json (option 1), a shell alias does not create an in-session slash command (option 2), and hooks fire on events rather than being invoked as named slash commands (option 3).
referenceText: Claude Code — custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands.

stem: You want Claude Code to automatically run a formatter after it edits any file, without relying on the model to remember. Which Claude Code feature enforces this deterministically?
options: A `PostToolUse` hook configured in settings that runs the formatter after matching tool calls
An instruction added to CLAUDE.md asking the model to format after edits
A slash command the user runs manually after each edit
Plan mode, which formats files as part of planning
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness executes deterministically on lifecycle events; a `PostToolUse` hook matching edit tools runs the formatter every time regardless of model behavior. A CLAUDE.md instruction is a suggestion the model may not always follow (option 1), a manual slash command isn't automatic (option 2), and plan mode is a read-only research mode that does not format files (option 3).
referenceText: Claude Code — PostToolUse hooks run deterministically after tool calls.

stem: A developer engages Claude Code's plan mode before a large refactor. What is the defining behavior of plan mode?
options: Claude researches and proposes a plan without making any file edits or running mutating commands until the plan is approved
Claude executes edits immediately but records them in a plan file for review
Claude runs only the test suite and reports failures
Claude switches to a smaller model to save tokens while planning
rationale: Plan mode is a read-only mode: Claude investigates the codebase and presents a proposed plan, deferring any edits or mutating actions until you approve. It does not make edits first (option 1), is not limited to running tests (option 2), and does not change the underlying model (option 3).
referenceText: Claude Code — plan mode researches and proposes without making changes.

stem: You are building an autonomous coding agent and want to reuse Claude Code's harness — its agent loop, tool execution, permissions, and context management — programmatically rather than reimplementing them. Which offering is designed for this?
options: The Claude Agent SDK
The Message Batches API
The Files API
Prompt caching
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the same agent harness that powers Claude Code (the agent loop, tool use, permission handling, and context management) for building custom agents. The Batches API is for bulk asynchronous requests (option 1), the Files API manages uploaded files (option 2), and prompt caching optimizes repeated context (option 3) — none provide an agent harness.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK — build agents on the Claude Code harness.

stem: When selecting a model from the models overview for a high-volume, latency-sensitive classification task where cost matters more than deep reasoning, which selection heuristic is most appropriate?
options: Choose a smaller, faster Haiku-tier model, since it optimizes for speed and cost on well-scoped tasks
Always choose the largest Opus-tier model to maximize accuracy regardless of task
Choose whichever model has the newest release date
Choose a model based solely on its context window size
rationale: Model selection should match task requirements; for high-volume, latency- and cost-sensitive, well-scoped tasks, a smaller/faster Haiku-tier model is the appropriate trade-off. Always maxing out on the largest model (option 1) wastes cost and latency, newest release date alone (option 2) ignores fit, and context window alone (option 3) is only one of several relevant dimensions.
referenceText: Models overview — match model tier to task speed/cost/intelligence needs.

stem: A developer sends a Messages API request whose only turn is a user message asking Claude to look up an order. Claude responds and the response object's stop_reason is "tool_use". What must the developer's application do next to continue the conversation correctly?
options: Append an assistant message containing the tool_use block, then append a new user message that contains a tool_result block referencing the same tool_use_id
Resend the original request unchanged, because tool_use means the request must be retried
Send a user message containing a tool_result block only, omitting the assistant turn that held the tool_use block
Set tool_choice to {"type": "none"} and resend so Claude answers without the tool
rationale: Correct: The conversation must preserve turn order. You echo back the assistant turn (with its tool_use block) and then supply a user turn whose tool_result block carries the matching tool_use_id and the tool's output, letting Claude continue. Distractors: retrying the same request loses the tool call and produces the same stop_reason; sending a tool_result without the preceding assistant tool_use breaks the required message structure and is rejected; forcing tool_choice none does not execute the requested tool and abandons the pending call.
referenceText: Anthropic tool use docs: after stop_reason tool_use, return the tool_use block in the assistant turn and a tool_result with the matching tool_use_id in the next user turn.

stem: In a tool definition passed to the Messages API, what is the purpose of the input_schema field?
options: It is a JSON Schema object describing the tool's expected input parameters so Claude generates a valid tool_use input
It contains the tool's executable source code that the API runs on Anthropic's servers
It stores the cached result of the last time the tool was called
It is a natural-language description shown to the end user in the client UI
rationale: Correct: input_schema is a JSON Schema definition of the tool's parameters (types, required fields), which Claude uses to structure the input it emits in a tool_use block. Distractors: the API never executes tool code — your application does; there is no result caching field inside a tool definition; the human-readable explanation belongs in the tool's description field, not input_schema.
referenceText: Tool use: each tool has name, description, and input_schema (JSON Schema) that defines the shape of the input Claude must produce.

stem: An application must guarantee that Claude calls the get_weather tool (and no other) on a particular request rather than optionally answering in prose. Which tool_choice value achieves this?
options: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
{"type": "auto"}
{"type": "none"}
{"type": "any"}
rationale: Correct: {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces Claude to invoke that specific named tool. Distractors: "auto" lets Claude decide whether to use any tool or answer directly; "none" forbids tool use entirely; "any" forces the model to use one of the available tools but not a specific one.
referenceText: tool_choice options: auto (model decides), any (must use some tool), tool (must use the named tool), none (no tools).

stem: After your application executes a tool and the tool raises an error, how should you communicate that failure back to Claude in the follow-up request?
options: Return a tool_result block with the matching tool_use_id and set is_error to true, with the error text in content
Omit the tool_result entirely so Claude infers the tool failed
Change the assistant's previous tool_use block to describe the error
Send a new system prompt instructing Claude that the tool failed
rationale: Correct: A tool_result block supports an is_error boolean; setting it true and placing the error message in content tells Claude the call failed so it can adjust. Distractors: omitting the tool_result leaves an unanswered tool_use and breaks structure; you must not rewrite the assistant's prior tool_use block; a system prompt is the wrong channel and does not link to the specific tool_use_id.
referenceText: tool_result blocks include tool_use_id, content, and an optional is_error flag to signal tool execution failures.

stem: When streaming a Messages API response over SSE, which event marks the beginning of each individual content block (such as a text block or a tool_use block) within the message?
options: content_block_start
message_start
message_delta
ping
rationale: Correct: content_block_start opens each content block; it is followed by content_block_delta events and a content_block_stop. Distractors: message_start fires once at the start of the whole message; message_delta carries top-level changes like stop_reason near the end; ping events are periodic keep-alives with no content meaning.
referenceText: Streaming event flow: message_start, then per block content_block_start / content_block_delta / content_block_stop, then message_delta and message_stop.

stem: While streaming a response that includes a tool call, which delta type carries the incrementally streamed JSON of the tool's input arguments?
options: input_json_delta
text_delta
thinking_delta
signature_delta
rationale: Correct: For tool_use blocks, the arguments stream as partial JSON via input_json_delta events that the client accumulates and parses once the block stops. Distractors: text_delta streams ordinary text content; thinking_delta streams extended-thinking content; signature_delta carries the cryptographic signature for a thinking block, not tool arguments.
referenceText: Streaming: tool_use input arrives as input_json_delta partial JSON fragments to be concatenated.

stem: A team wants to process 40,000 independent document-classification prompts overnight and does not need immediate responses. Which statements about using the Message Batches API for this are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Batches are processed asynchronously, with most completing well within the 24-hour target window
Batch processing is offered at a discount (roughly 50%) compared to standard per-request pricing
Batches guarantee sub-second latency for each request, like synchronous calls
Results must be retrieved as a single JSON field in the create-batch HTTP response body
Each request in a batch must depend on the output of the previous request in order
rationale: Correct: The Batches API runs jobs asynchronously with a 24-hour completion target and prices batch requests at a discount (about 50% off). Distractors: batches are explicitly not for low-latency needs; results are fetched later from a results URL/endpoint once the batch ends, not returned inline at creation; batch requests are independent and processed in parallel, not chained sequentially.
referenceText: Message Batches API: asynchronous processing within 24 hours at ~50% cost savings; results retrieved from the batch's results endpoint after completion.

stem: A developer prepends a large, unchanging 20,000-token knowledge base to every request and wants repeated requests to be cheaper and faster. How does prompt caching mark which prefix to cache?
options: By placing a cache_control breakpoint with {"type": "ephemeral"} on the last content block of the reusable prefix
By setting a top-level cache: true parameter on the request
By sending the knowledge base once to a separate /cache endpoint before messaging
By adding the word CACHE at the start of the system prompt
rationale: Correct: You mark a cache breakpoint by adding cache_control with type ephemeral to the content block at the end of the prefix you want cached; everything up to that point is eligible for caching. Distractors: there is no top-level cache boolean, no separate cache-upload endpoint, and no magic keyword — caching is controlled per content block via cache_control.
referenceText: Prompt caching: add cache_control (type ephemeral) to content blocks to define cache breakpoints for reusable prefixes.

stem: By default, how long does a prompt cache entry created with a standard ephemeral cache_control breakpoint remain valid before it expires if not refreshed?
options: About 5 minutes, refreshed each time the cached prefix is used
Exactly 24 hours regardless of usage
Until the API key is rotated
Permanently, until the developer deletes it via an API call
rationale: Correct: The default ephemeral cache has roughly a 5-minute time-to-live that resets each time the cached content is read (a longer 1-hour TTL is available as an option). Distractors: 24 hours is not the default TTL; caches are not tied to API key rotation; ephemeral caches expire automatically and cannot be made permanent or manually deleted.
referenceText: Prompt caching default TTL is ~5 minutes, refreshed on cache hits; a 1-hour TTL option also exists.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking on a request. Which parameter controls how many tokens Claude may spend on its internal reasoning before producing the final answer?
options: thinking.budget_tokens within the thinking configuration object
temperature set above 1.0
top_k raised to a large value
stop_sequences containing the word THINK
rationale: Correct: Extended thinking is enabled with a thinking object of type enabled and a budget_tokens value that caps the reasoning tokens. Distractors: temperature and top_k affect sampling, not a reasoning budget; stop_sequences terminate generation on a string and have nothing to do with thinking budget.
referenceText: Extended thinking: set thinking to {type: enabled, budget_tokens: N} to allocate the reasoning token budget.

stem: A latency-sensitive, high-volume classification feature needs the fastest, most cost-efficient Claude model while accepting somewhat lower reasoning depth. Consulting the models overview, which family is the best fit?
options: A Haiku model
An Opus model
Whichever model has the largest parameter count regardless of speed
A model chosen at random per request to balance load
rationale: Correct: Haiku models are positioned as the fastest and most cost-effective tier, ideal for high-throughput, latency-sensitive tasks. Distractors: Opus is the most capable but slower and costlier, over-provisioned for simple classification; picking purely on size ignores the documented speed/cost tradeoffs; random model selection produces inconsistent behavior and cost.
referenceText: Models overview: Haiku is optimized for speed and cost; Opus for the most complex reasoning; choose per task requirements.

stem: A developer wants to build an autonomous coding agent that plans, edits files, runs commands, and iterates, reusing the same agent harness that powers Claude Code. Which Anthropic offering is designed for this?
options: The Claude Agent SDK
The Message Batches API
Prompt caching
The token counting endpoint
rationale: Correct: The Claude Agent SDK exposes the agent loop, tool execution, and context management used by Claude Code so developers can build custom agents. Distractors: the Batches API is for bulk asynchronous inference; prompt caching optimizes repeated prefixes; the token counting endpoint only estimates token usage — none of these provide an agent harness.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK: build agents on the same infrastructure as Claude Code, including tool use and the agent loop.

stem: A team wants project-specific conventions (build commands, code style, architectural notes) to be automatically available to Claude Code every session without pasting them each time. What is the intended mechanism?
options: Commit a CLAUDE.md memory file at the project root, which Claude Code loads into context automatically
Set the conventions as environment variables in the shell profile
Rename the repository so the conventions appear in the folder name
Add the conventions only to the git commit message of the first commit
rationale: Correct: CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's memory file; a project-root CLAUDE.md is pulled into context each session so shared conventions persist. Distractors: environment variables are not read as project memory; a folder name cannot convey guidelines; a commit message is not loaded as ongoing context.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: CLAUDE.md files (project and user scope) are automatically loaded to provide persistent project context.

stem: A developer wants to package a frequently used, parameterized prompt (e.g. "/fix-issue <number>") so any teammate can invoke it by name in Claude Code. What is the correct approach?
options: Create a custom slash command as a Markdown file in the .claude/commands directory
Hard-code the prompt into the model weights
Add it as a new event in the streaming SSE specification
Register it as an input_schema on a Messages API tool
rationale: Correct: Custom slash commands are defined as Markdown files under .claude/commands (project) or the user commands directory, and can accept arguments. Distractors: model weights are fixed and not user-editable; SSE events are a transport detail, not a place for prompts; a tool input_schema defines tool parameters for the API, not a reusable Claude Code command.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: define reusable commands as Markdown files in .claude/commands, supporting arguments.

stem: A team wants to automatically run a formatter after Claude Code edits any file, and to block edits to a protected directory. Which Claude Code feature lets them run their own shell commands at defined points in the agent's lifecycle?
options: Hooks configured in settings.json
The CLAUDE.md memory file
Plan mode
The tool_choice parameter
rationale: Correct: Hooks are user-defined shell commands that execute at lifecycle events (for example PreToolUse and PostToolUse), enabling auto-formatting or blocking actions. Distractors: CLAUDE.md provides context but cannot execute commands or enforce policy deterministically; plan mode gates execution but is not a command-runner for arbitrary events; tool_choice is a Messages API parameter unrelated to Claude Code lifecycle automation.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks: shell commands triggered at events like PreToolUse/PostToolUse to enforce or automate behavior.

stem: A developer starts a risky refactor in Claude Code and wants Claude to research the codebase and propose a step-by-step approach for approval WITHOUT making any file changes yet. Which capability fits this need?
options: Plan mode, which lets Claude analyze and propose a plan before any edits are executed
Extended thinking budget_tokens set to zero
The Message Batches API
Setting tool_choice to any
rationale: Correct: Plan mode is a read-only mode where Claude Code investigates and presents a plan for the user to approve before it makes changes. Distractors: a zero thinking budget just disables extended reasoning, it does not gate file edits; the Batches API is for bulk API inference; tool_choice any forces tool use rather than preventing edits.
referenceText: Claude Code plan mode: a read-only planning mode that proposes an approach before making changes.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, a server exposes several primitives to clients. Which of the following are core MCP primitives an application can consume? (Select 3.)
options: Tools (model-invocable functions the server can execute)
Resources (data the server exposes for context, addressed by URI)
Prompts (reusable prompt templates the server offers)
Weights (the client's fine-tuned model parameters synced to the server)
Invoices (per-request billing records the server must return)
rationale: Correct: MCP defines tools (executable functions), resources (URI-addressed context data), and prompts (reusable templates) as the primitives a server exposes. Distractors: MCP does not transfer model weights between client and server, and billing/invoicing is not an MCP primitive.
referenceText: MCP core building blocks include tools, resources, and prompts exposed by servers to clients.

stem: In MCP, which primitive is intended to be application-controlled context data (for example, file contents or database rows) exposed to the client and typically selected by the user or app rather than autonomously invoked by the model?
options: Resources
Tools
Sampling
Transports
rationale: Correct: Resources represent data (identified by URIs) that a server exposes for context; they are typically application- or user-selected rather than model-invoked. Distractors: tools are model-invoked functions with side effects; sampling is the server requesting an LLM completion through the client; transports (stdio, HTTP) are how messages move, not a context primitive.
referenceText: MCP resources: expose data/content identified by URIs for use as model context, generally application-controlled.

stem: A developer inspects a Messages API response and sees stop_reason set to "max_tokens". What does this indicate?
options: Generation stopped because it reached the max_tokens limit, so the output may be truncated mid-response
Claude finished its answer naturally and the turn is complete
Claude is pausing to call a tool and awaits a tool_result
The request hit a stop_sequence that was supplied by the developer
rationale: Correct: stop_reason max_tokens means the model hit the configured output token cap and the content may be cut off; raising max_tokens or continuing generation may be needed. Distractors: a natural completion is end_turn; a pending tool call is tool_use; hitting a developer stop string is stop_sequence.
referenceText: Messages API stop_reason values include end_turn, max_tokens, stop_sequence, and tool_use.

stem: A developer builds a chat feature and wants to give Claude persistent instructions about its role and constraints that are not part of the back-and-forth turns. Where should these instructions go in a Messages API request?
options: In the top-level system parameter
As an extra element in the messages array with role "system"
Inside each tool's input_schema
In the metadata parameter
rationale: Correct: The Messages API takes role/behavior instructions in the dedicated top-level system parameter, separate from the messages array. Distractors: the messages array accepts only user and assistant roles — there is no system role element there; input_schema defines tool parameters; metadata carries opaque identifiers like user_id, not behavioral instructions.
referenceText: Messages API: the system prompt is provided via the top-level system parameter; messages contain user and assistant turns only.

stem: A developer provides three tools and, on a single turn, Claude returns two separate tool_use blocks in one assistant message. How should the application respond to continue correctly?
options: Execute both tools and return both tool_results (each with its own tool_use_id) in the content of a single following user message
Return only the first tool_result and ignore the second tool_use block
Send two separate requests, one tool_result per request, resetting the conversation each time
Reject the response because Claude may only emit one tool_use block per turn
rationale: Correct: When Claude issues multiple (parallel) tool_use blocks in one turn, the app runs each tool and returns all corresponding tool_result blocks — each matched by tool_use_id — within one user message. Distractors: dropping a tool_result leaves an unanswered tool call; splitting into separate reset conversations loses context and pairing; multiple tool_use blocks per turn are explicitly supported, not an error.
referenceText: Tool use supports parallel tool calls: return all tool_result blocks (matched by tool_use_id) in a single user turn.

stem: A developer is adding a multi-turn chat feature on top of the Messages API. To make Claude aware of everything said so far in the conversation, what must the developer do on each request?
options: Store conversation state server-side using a conversation_id and send only the newest user message
Rely on the system prompt to persist prior turns, which the API reconstructs automatically
Resend the full conversation history as an alternating sequence of user and assistant turns in the messages array, because the API is stateless
Enable prompt caching so history is retained server-side for 24 hours and never needs resending
rationale: The Messages API is stateless: it holds no memory of prior requests, so the client must resend the entire prior exchange (alternating user/assistant turns) in the messages array each call. There is no conversation_id that persists turns server-side, the system prompt does not reconstruct history, and prompt caching only lowers the cost of re-processing repeated prefix tokens — it does not remove the requirement to resend the messages.
referenceText: Messages API — conversations are stateless; send the full message history on each request.

stem: When defining a tool that Claude can call, what is the purpose of the tool's input_schema field?
options: It contains the executable code the API runs on the server when the tool is invoked
It is a JSON Schema object describing the expected parameters (names, types, required fields) so Claude generates valid tool_use inputs
It specifies the natural-language format Claude must follow in its final text reply after the tool runs
It is a JSON Schema describing the tool's return value so the API can validate the tool_result before sending it back
rationale: input_schema is a JSON Schema that documents the shape of the tool's inputs; Claude uses it to produce well-formed tool_use blocks. The API never executes tool code (the developer does that), input_schema does not govern Claude's prose output format, and it describes inputs — not the tool's return value.
referenceText: Tool use overview — each tool definition includes name, description, and an input_schema (JSON Schema).

stem: A developer wants to force Claude to call the tool named get_weather on the next turn rather than reply with text. Which tool_choice value accomplishes this?
options: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
{"type": "auto"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "required", "name": "get_weather"}
rationale: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"} forces that specific tool. "auto" lets Claude decide whether to use any tool or answer in text, "any" forces the use of some tool but not a specific one, and "required" is not a valid tool_choice type in the Messages API.
referenceText: Tool use — tool_choice supports auto, any, and tool (with a name) to force a specific tool.

stem: Claude responds with a tool_use block whose id is "toolu_01A". After the developer runs the tool, how must the result be returned to Claude?
options: As a system message containing the output keyed by the tool's name
As a new assistant message containing a tool_result block that references the tool by name
By appending the output as plain text to Claude's previous assistant message
As a new user message containing a tool_result block whose tool_use_id equals "toolu_01A"
rationale: Tool output is returned in the next user turn as a tool_result content block, and it must set tool_use_id to the id of the corresponding tool_use block ("toolu_01A") so Claude can match result to request. It is not sent as a system message, not as an assistant message, is keyed by id rather than name, and cannot be appended as plain text.
referenceText: Tool use — return results in a user message tool_result block with a matching tool_use_id.

stem: A developer is implementing the agentic tool-use loop with the Messages API. Which statements about that loop are correct? (Select 2.)
options: The API executes the requested tool automatically on its servers and returns the tool's output in the same response
When Claude decides to use a tool, the response's stop_reason is "tool_use"
A tool_use block is returned without an id, so results are matched by tool name
The developer runs the tool and sends the output back in a tool_result block within a new user message
Tool definitions are sent once and then stored server-side for the life of the conversation
rationale: Claude signals a tool call by ending with stop_reason "tool_use", and the developer must execute the tool and return its output in a tool_result block in a following user message. The API does not execute tools itself, every tool_use block carries an id used for matching, and because the API is stateless the tools must be included on each request rather than stored server-side.
referenceText: Tool use overview — stop_reason tool_use signals a tool call the client executes and returns via tool_result.

stem: A developer is consuming a streaming Messages API response over server-sent events (SSE). Which event type carries the incremental chunks of generated text?
options: message_start
content_block_delta
content_block_start
message_delta
rationale: content_block_delta events carry the incremental token/text updates (e.g., text_delta) as the model generates them. message_start opens the stream with an initial message shell, content_block_start signals the beginning of a block, and message_delta carries top-level changes such as the final stop_reason — not the streamed text itself.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — content_block_delta events deliver incremental text via text_delta.

stem: Which workload is the best fit for the Message Batches API rather than standard synchronous requests?
options: A live customer-support chatbot that must respond within one second
Streaming a long-form article to a user's browser as each token is produced
Guaranteeing a forced tool call on every interactive request
Classifying 50,000 archived documents overnight, where lower per-request cost matters more than immediate responses
rationale: The Batches API is built for large, latency-tolerant, high-volume jobs processed asynchronously at reduced cost — a perfect match for tens of thousands of overnight classifications. Interactive chatbots and token streaming require synchronous, low-latency responses, and forcing tool calls is a tool_choice concern unrelated to batching.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous, high-throughput processing for non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: Which statements accurately describe the Message Batches API? (Select 2.)
options: Batches are processed asynchronously and complete within 24 hours, often sooner
Each request in a batch is guaranteed to return within 60 seconds
Batch requests are billed at a discount relative to standard synchronous requests
Batches stream partial token output over SSE as each request finishes
There is no way to check a batch's status; results simply appear when done
rationale: Batches are asynchronous and complete within a 24-hour target window (frequently faster) and are offered at a cost discount versus synchronous requests. There is no 60-second per-request guarantee, batches do not stream tokens over SSE, and developers can poll/retrieve batch status and results.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous processing within 24 hours at a discounted price.

stem: A developer repeatedly sends the same large system prompt and reference document across many requests and enables prompt caching. How does prompt caching reduce cost and latency?
options: By marking a prefix with cache_control, the cached input tokens are re-read at a reduced price and lower latency on subsequent requests within the cache lifetime
By compressing the response payload so fewer output tokens are billed
By storing the model's final answer and returning it verbatim when the same prompt is sent again
By automatically summarizing older conversation turns to fit the context window
rationale: Prompt caching stores a marked prefix of input tokens so that repeated requests read those tokens at a reduced rate with lower latency during the cache lifetime. It caches inputs, not outputs — it does not compress responses, does not return a stored final answer, and does not summarize history.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache_control marks reusable prefixes; cached input tokens cost less on cache hits.

stem: A team enables prompt caching but observes frequent cache misses. Which practices will maximize cache hits? (Select 2.)
options: Place large, static content (system prompt, tool definitions, long documents) at the start of the prompt and keep it byte-identical across requests, marking the boundary with cache_control
Prepend a unique request timestamp to the top of the system prompt on every call
Position dynamic, user-specific content after the cached prefix so the static prefix stays stable
Randomize the order of tool definitions on each request to spread cache load
Keep every cached segment below the minimum cacheable token threshold
rationale: Caching keys on an exact, stable prefix, so static content should sit at the front, be byte-identical, and be marked with cache_control, while variable content goes after the cached prefix. Adding a per-request timestamp or reordering tools changes the prefix and breaks the cache, and content below the minimum cacheable token count cannot be cached at all.
referenceText: Prompt caching best practices — keep static prefixes stable and place variable content after them.

stem: What does enabling extended thinking on a Messages API request do?
options: It automatically doubles the maximum output length of every response
It disables tool use so the model can focus purely on reasoning
It allocates a token budget for internal reasoning and returns thinking content blocks before the final answer to improve performance on complex tasks
It caches the model's reasoning so identical prompts skip computation entirely
rationale: Extended thinking gives the model a configurable token budget to reason internally, surfacing thinking content blocks before the final answer and improving results on hard, multi-step problems. It does not simply double output length, does not disable tool use (thinking can be combined with tools), and is unrelated to caching reasoning across requests.
referenceText: Extended thinking — allocate a thinking budget; the model returns thinking blocks before its answer.

stem: A response from Claude is cut off mid-sentence, and the response object reports stop_reason "max_tokens". What is the correct interpretation and remedy?
options: The output reached the request's max_tokens limit; raise max_tokens (or ask for shorter output), and to continue, feed the partial output back and ask the model to resume
The model refused the request for safety reasons; rephrase the prompt to comply with policy
A tool call is pending; return a tool_result block to let generation continue
The request was rate-limited; retry the identical request with exponential backoff
rationale: stop_reason "max_tokens" means generation stopped because it hit the output token cap, so the fix is to raise max_tokens or request briefer output, and to continue you resend the truncated content and ask Claude to continue. A safety stop, a pending tool call (stop_reason tool_use), and rate limiting (an HTTP 429, not a stop_reason) are different conditions.
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason max_tokens indicates the output token limit was reached.

stem: A developer needs the most cost-efficient, lowest-latency Claude model to handle very high-volume, simple text classification. Which model family is the best starting point?
options: Opus, because it is the most capable model
Haiku, because it is optimized for speed and cost on straightforward tasks
Sonnet, because it is the only model that supports tool use
An extended-thinking-only model that always reasons at length before answering
rationale: Haiku is the fastest and most cost-efficient family, ideal for high-volume, straightforward tasks like simple classification. Opus is the most capable but costlier and slower for such simple work, Sonnet is not the only tool-use-capable model, and there is no 'extended-thinking-only' family — extended thinking is a feature, not a separate model tier.
referenceText: Models overview — Haiku targets speed and cost-efficiency; Opus targets maximum capability.

stem: What is the primary purpose of the Claude Agent SDK?
options: It provides a framework for building autonomous agents that run an agent loop with tools, context management, and permission controls on top of Claude
It is a UI component library for rendering chat bubbles and message threads in web apps
It replaces the Messages API with a fine-tuning interface for custom models
It is a hosted vector database service for retrieval-augmented generation
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK is a framework for building agents — it manages the agent loop, tool calling, context, and permissions so developers can build autonomous workflows on Claude. It is not a front-end component library, not a fine-tuning replacement for the Messages API, and not a vector database.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK overview — build agents with the agent loop, tools, and context/permission management.

stem: What is the role of a CLAUDE.md file placed at the root of a repository used with Claude Code?
options: It is a lockfile that pins the Claude model version the project must use
It stores API keys and secrets so the session can authenticate
It provides persistent project context (conventions, common commands, architecture notes) that Claude Code automatically loads into the session
It defines CI pipeline steps that run before each Claude Code command
rationale: CLAUDE.md is a memory file Claude Code loads automatically to give the session durable project context such as coding conventions, key commands, and architecture. It is not a model-version lockfile, is not the place for secrets, and does not define CI steps.
referenceText: Claude Code memory — CLAUDE.md supplies persistent project context loaded into each session.

stem: A team wants a reusable /deploy command in Claude Code that runs a predefined prompt. How are custom slash commands defined?
options: As Markdown files placed in a commands directory (for example .claude/commands), where the filename becomes the command name
As entries under a "claude-commands" key in package.json
As environment variables prefixed with CLAUDE_CMD_
As compiled plugins that must first be published to a public registry
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in a commands directory (such as .claude/commands), and the file's name becomes the invoked command name. They are not configured through package.json, environment variables, or a mandatory published-plugin pipeline.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands — define custom commands as Markdown files in a commands directory.

stem: A developer wants a linter to run automatically every time Claude Code edits a file, without manual intervention. Which Claude Code feature should they use?
options: A slash command the user must type after each edit
Hooks — shell commands configured to run automatically on events such as after a file-editing tool executes
Plan mode, which runs the linter before applying any edits
CLAUDE.md, by describing in prose that the linter should run after edits
rationale: Hooks are user-defined shell commands that Claude Code runs automatically on lifecycle events (for example, after a tool such as an edit runs), making them the right mechanism for auto-linting. A slash command requires manual invocation, plan mode gates edits rather than post-processing them, and prose in CLAUDE.md is guidance the model may follow but is not a guaranteed automated trigger.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks — run shell commands automatically on tool/lifecycle events like PostToolUse.

stem: What is the purpose of plan mode in Claude Code?
options: It lets Claude research the task and present a plan for the user's approval without making file edits or running mutating commands until the plan is accepted
It parallelizes edits across multiple files to complete work faster
It permanently disables all tool use for the remainder of the session
It compresses the conversation to save tokens before executing changes
rationale: Plan mode is a read-only research-and-propose phase: Claude investigates and drafts a plan but does not edit files or run mutating actions until the user approves. It is not about parallelizing edits, does not permanently disable tools, and is unrelated to token-saving conversation compression.
referenceText: Claude Code workflows — plan mode proposes a plan for approval before making changes.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which statements about the core primitives are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Tools are model-controlled: the LLM invokes them to take actions or fetch computed results
Resources are executed by the model to perform side effects on the host system
Resources are application-controlled data identified by URIs that a server exposes for the client to read as context
Tools are read-only, URI-addressable documents that only the user can open
Prompts are model-controlled functions that execute arbitrary code on the server
rationale: In MCP, tools are model-controlled actions the LLM chooses to call, and resources are application-controlled, URI-identified data exposed to provide context. Resources are read, not executed; tools are executable actions rather than read-only documents; and prompts are user-controlled reusable templates, not code-executing functions.
referenceText: MCP concepts — tools are model-controlled actions; resources are application-controlled context; prompts are user-controlled templates.

stem: A developer sends a Messages API request and the response comes back with "stop_reason": "tool_use". What is the correct next step to continue the conversation?
options: Resend the identical request with tool_choice set to "none" so the model finishes its answer
Append the assistant's tool_use content block to the messages, then send a new user message containing a tool_result block whose tool_use_id matches the tool_use id
Immediately retry the request because stop_reason "tool_use" indicates a transient server error
Discard the assistant turn and start a fresh conversation, passing the tool output in the system prompt
rationale: When stop_reason is "tool_use", the model is requesting a tool call. You execute the tool locally and return the outcome in a new user message using a tool_result block whose tool_use_id equals the id from the tool_use block; the prior assistant turn (including the tool_use block) must remain in the messages array. Setting tool_choice to "none" or retrying does not deliver the tool output. Putting results in the system prompt breaks the required tool_use_id linkage.
referenceText: After receiving stop_reason tool_use, extract the tool name and input, run the tool, and return a tool_result content block with a matching tool_use_id in a subsequent user message.

stem: When defining a custom tool for the Messages API, which field describes the parameters the tool accepts and is used by Claude to structure its tool_use input?
options: parameters, expressed as an OpenAPI path object
input_schema, expressed as a JSON Schema object
arguments, expressed as a Python type hint string
schema_definition, expressed as a Protobuf message
rationale: Each tool definition includes name, description, and input_schema, where input_schema is a JSON Schema object describing the expected parameters. Claude uses it to produce a valid tool_use input. The other field names (parameters, arguments, schema_definition) and formats (OpenAPI, type hints, Protobuf) are not what the Messages API tool definition uses.
referenceText: Tool definitions include an input_schema field: a JSON Schema object defining the parameters expected by the tool.

stem: A developer wants to guarantee that Claude calls a specific tool named get_weather on the next turn rather than answering in free text. Which tool_choice value accomplishes this?
options: {"type": "auto"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
{"type": "none"}
rationale: tool_choice of type "tool" with a name forces the model to invoke that specific named tool. type "auto" lets the model decide whether to use any tool; type "any" forces use of some tool but not a specific one; type "none" prevents tool use entirely.
referenceText: Use tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": "..."} to force Claude to use a specific tool; "any" forces one of any tool, "auto" is the default, and "none" disables tools.

stem: In a streaming (SSE) Messages API response, which event carries the incremental text or JSON fragments that make up the model's output?
options: message_start
content_block_delta
message_stop
ping
rationale: content_block_delta events carry incremental updates such as text_delta or input_json_delta for a content block. message_start signals the start of the overall message with an empty content array; message_stop signals the end; ping events are periodic keep-alives with no content.
referenceText: Each content block is streamed as content_block_start, one or more content_block_delta events (e.g., text_delta, input_json_delta), and a content_block_stop.

stem: A team runs a large offline classification job that is not latency-sensitive. Which characteristic of the Message Batches API makes it the right choice compared to standard synchronous requests?
options: It returns every result in under one second by reserving dedicated capacity
It processes requests asynchronously, typically completing within 24 hours, at a reduced cost per token
It guarantees the model will never return a refusal for batched requests
It automatically enables extended thinking on all requests in the batch
rationale: The Message Batches API processes groups of requests asynchronously, with batches completing within 24 hours (often sooner), at a significant cost discount versus standard requests. It is not low-latency, does not change refusal behavior, and does not force extended thinking on.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously, completing within 24 hours at a 50% discount compared to standard API pricing.

stem: Which of the following are true about prompt caching with the Messages API? (Select 2.)
options: You mark the end of cacheable content by adding a cache_control block with type "ephemeral"
Cache read (hit) tokens are billed at a lower rate than uncached base input tokens
Caching requires no minimum prompt length; even a 10-token prompt is cached
A cached prompt persists indefinitely until you explicitly call a delete-cache endpoint
Prompt caching can only be applied to the system prompt, never to tools or messages
rationale: Cache breakpoints are set by attaching cache_control with type "ephemeral" to a content block, and cache reads are billed at a fraction (about 10%) of base input token cost. There is a minimum cacheable length (e.g., 1024 tokens for larger models), the cache has a limited TTL (default 5 minutes, refreshed on use) rather than living forever, and cache breakpoints can be placed on tools, system, and messages content.
referenceText: Add cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"} to designate a cache breakpoint; cache hits are billed at reduced cost and entries have a minimum length and a limited lifetime.

stem: A developer enables extended thinking on a request. Which parameter controls how many tokens Claude may spend on internal reasoning before producing its final answer?
options: thinking.budget_tokens
max_tokens applied only to reasoning
reasoning_effort set to "high"
temperature raised above 1.0
rationale: Extended thinking is configured with a thinking object of type "enabled" and a budget_tokens value that caps the reasoning tokens (and must be at least 1024). max_tokens governs total output, not a separate reasoning budget; reasoning_effort is not the Messages API parameter for this; temperature does not control thinking length.
referenceText: Enable extended thinking with thinking: {"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": N}, where budget_tokens must be >= 1024 and less than max_tokens.

stem: Where does Claude Code look for project-level instructions and context that should be automatically loaded at the start of a session in a repository?
options: A .clauderc JSON file in the user's home directory
A CLAUDE.md memory file at the root of the project
The description field of the most recent git commit
An environment variable named CLAUDE_CONTEXT
rationale: Claude Code reads a CLAUDE.md memory file (project memory at the repo root, plus user memory at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) to load persistent instructions and context. .clauderc, commit messages, and a CLAUDE_CONTEXT env var are not the memory mechanism.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md files provide project and user memory that Claude Code automatically pulls into context at session start.

stem: A developer wants to add a reusable custom slash command called /review-pr to Claude Code that is shared with the whole team via the repository. Where should the command be defined?
options: As a Markdown file in the project's .claude/commands/ directory
As a JSON entry in the global ~/.claude.json config
As a shell alias in the developer's ~/.bashrc
As a comment block at the top of CLAUDE.md
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files; placing review-pr.md in the project's .claude/commands/ directory makes /review-pr available to everyone who checks out the repo (personal commands go in ~/.claude/commands/). A global JSON config, a shell alias, or a CLAUDE.md comment do not define slash commands.
referenceText: Project slash commands are Markdown files stored in .claude/commands/ and are shared with the team through the repository; personal commands live in ~/.claude/commands/.

stem: A team wants Claude Code to automatically run a secret-scanner and block any edit that would write an API key to disk, before the file is written. Which Claude Code hook event should they configure?
options: PostToolUse, so the scan runs after the file is written
PreToolUse, so the scan can inspect and block the tool call before it executes
Stop, so the scan runs when the session ends
Notification, so the scan runs when Claude sends a notification
rationale: PreToolUse hooks run before a tool executes and can block the call (e.g., by returning a blocking decision or exit code 2), which is exactly what is needed to prevent a write. PostToolUse runs after the action has already happened; Stop runs at session end; Notification fires on notifications, none of which can prevent the write.
referenceText: PreToolUse hooks run before a tool call and can approve or block it; PostToolUse runs after a tool completes.

stem: What is the primary purpose of plan mode in Claude Code?
options: It lets Claude research the codebase and present a proposed plan without making file edits or running mutating commands until you approve
It compresses the conversation history to save tokens during long sessions
It permanently disables all tool use for the remainder of the session
It automatically commits and pushes changes after each completed step
rationale: Plan mode is a read-only mode in which Claude investigates and proposes a plan for approval before taking any mutating action, which is useful for reviewing an approach before code is changed. It is not a context-compaction feature, does not permanently disable tools, and does not auto-commit.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude analyze and plan without modifying files or executing changes until you approve the proposed plan.

stem: In the Model Context Protocol, which primitive is model-controlled, meaning Claude can decide to invoke it to perform an action during a conversation?
options: Resources
Prompts
Tools
Roots
rationale: MCP tools are model-controlled: the model may choose to call them to take actions or fetch computed results.
