Resources are application-controlled data the host exposes to context; prompts are user-controlled templates typically surfaced as user-invoked commands; roots define filesystem boundaries and are not model-invoked actions.
referenceText: Tools are model-controlled functions that the LLM can call; resources are application-controlled and prompts are user-controlled.

stem: An MCP server exposes a set of files and database rows that a host application can read and attach to the model's context. Which MCP primitive represents this kind of application-controlled, read-oriented data?
options: Tools
Resources
Prompts
Sampling
rationale: Resources are application-controlled pieces of contextual data (identified by URIs) that the host can read and provide to the model, such as files or database records. Tools perform actions, prompts are user-invoked templates, and sampling is a server-initiated request for the host to run an LLM completion.
referenceText: Resources expose data and content from servers that can be read by clients and used as context; they are application-controlled.

stem: In MCP, prompts are described as user-controlled. In a host like Claude Code, how are MCP prompts most commonly surfaced to the user?
options: As background tasks that run automatically on every model turn
As user-invocable commands (for example, slash commands) that the user explicitly triggers
As silent system prompts the user can never see or select
As tools the model calls without any user involvement
rationale: MCP prompts are user-controlled templates that hosts typically surface as explicit user actions, such as slash commands the user selects. They are not automatic background tasks, not hidden system prompts, and not model-invoked tools (which are model-controlled).
referenceText: Prompts are user-controlled, reusable templates that servers expose and users explicitly select, often surfaced as slash commands.

stem: When returning a tool result to Claude that represents a failed tool execution, how should the tool_result content block signal the error so the model can react appropriately?
options: Set the top-level stop_reason of the request to "error"
Include "is_error": true on the tool_result block along with the error content
Omit the tool_use_id so the model ignores the failed call
Change the message role to "system" for that turn
rationale: A tool_result block can set is_error to true and include the error text so the model knows the tool failed and can adjust. stop_reason is set by the API, not the caller; omitting tool_use_id breaks the required linkage to the tool_use call; and tool_result blocks are returned in a user-role message, not a system message.
referenceText: To report a tool failure, return a tool_result content block with is_error: true and the error details, keeping the matching tool_use_id.

stem: Which statements accurately describe the Messages API request structure? (Select 2.)
options: The messages array must alternate roles, and the final message is typically from the user
max_tokens specifies the maximum number of tokens to generate in the response and is required
System-level instructions must be included as the first element of the messages array with role "system"
Each request may contain at most one content block per message
The model parameter is optional and defaults to the newest available model
rationale: The messages array uses alternating user/assistant roles and generally ends with a user turn, and max_tokens is a required parameter capping generated output. System instructions go in the top-level system parameter, not as a message with role "system"; a message's content can contain multiple content blocks; and model is a required parameter with no implicit default.
referenceText: Requests require model, max_tokens, and messages; system instructions use the top-level system parameter and messages alternate between user and assistant roles.

stem: A developer must choose a Claude model for a high-volume, latency-sensitive workflow where cost per request matters more than maximum reasoning depth. According to Anthropic's models guidance, which choice reflects the right trade-off?
options: Always select the largest Opus-tier model because it minimizes total cost
Select a smaller, faster Haiku-tier model, accepting less depth in exchange for lower cost and latency
Select any model and set max_tokens to 0 to eliminate cost
Model choice is irrelevant because all Claude models share identical price and latency
rationale: Anthropic's guidance is to match the model to the task: smaller, faster models (Haiku tier) suit high-volume, latency- and cost-sensitive tasks, while larger models (Opus tier) suit the most complex reasoning. Opus is not the cheapest, max_tokens of 0 produces no output, and the models differ meaningfully in price and latency.
referenceText: Choose the model that fits the task: faster, more cost-effective models for high-volume simple tasks and larger models for complex reasoning.

stem: What does the Claude Agent SDK primarily provide to developers?
options: A hosted UI theme editor for customizing the Claude.ai web interface
A framework for building autonomous agents on the same harness that powers Claude Code, handling the agent loop, tools, and context management
A drop-in replacement for the Messages API that removes the need for an API key
A billing dashboard for tracking token usage across an organization
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK gives developers the building blocks used by Claude Code to build custom agents, including the agent loop, tool integration (including MCP), and context management. It is not a UI theme editor, not an authentication-free API replacement, and not a billing dashboard.
referenceText: The Claude Agent SDK provides the infrastructure behind Claude Code for building agents, including the agent loop, tool use, and context management.

stem: While streaming a response that includes a tool call, how are the arguments for the tool_use block delivered across the SSE stream?
options: As a single complete JSON object in the content_block_start event
As incremental input_json_delta fragments in content_block_delta events that must be accumulated and parsed once the block stops
As plain text_delta events identical to normal message text
In the final message_stop event as a base64-encoded blob
rationale: For a streaming tool_use block, the input is emitted as partial JSON via input_json_delta events inside content_block_delta; the client concatenates these fragments and parses the complete JSON after content_block_stop. The full input is not delivered in content_block_start, is not text_delta, and is not a blob in message_stop.
referenceText: Tool use inputs stream as input_json_delta fragments within content_block_delta events, which are accumulated into the final JSON.

stem: A developer using the Message Batches API submits a batch and needs to retrieve outputs. Which statement correctly describes how batch results are handled?
options: Results are streamed back synchronously over SSE as each request completes
The batch is processed and, once its processing_status is ended, results are retrieved from the batch's results URL
Results are emailed to the account owner and are not accessible via the API
Each request in the batch must be polled individually at its own separate endpoint
rationale: You poll the batch object until its processing_status becomes ended, then fetch the per-request results from the batch's results_url (a JSONL stream of results). Batches are asynchronous, not synchronous SSE; results are retrieved via the API, not email; and results are fetched from the batch's single results endpoint rather than a separate endpoint per request.
referenceText: Poll the message batch until processing_status is ended, then download results from the results_url as JSONL.

stem: A developer's Messages API call returns a response whose top-level stop_reason is "max_tokens" instead of "end_turn". The model's text output is cut off mid-sentence. What is the most appropriate debugging step?
options: Lower the temperature to 0 so the model produces shorter, deterministic output
Switch tool_choice to {"type": "none"} to prevent the truncation
Increase the max_tokens value in the request so the model has room to finish, then retry
Add a stop_sequences entry for the cut-off word so the API completes the sentence
rationale: stop_reason "max_tokens" means generation stopped because it hit the token cap set by max_tokens, not because the model finished. Raising max_tokens (within the model's output limit) lets it complete. Temperature affects randomness, not length. tool_choice controls tool invocation, not text length. stop_sequences would cause earlier truncation, not completion.
referenceText: stop_reason "max_tokens" indicates the model stopped because it reached the maximum number of tokens allowed by the max_tokens parameter.

stem: While testing tool use, a developer sends a tool_use response back but the API returns a 400 error stating the tool_result content does not match. Which requirement is most likely being violated?
options: The tool_result must be placed in an assistant-role message immediately after the tool_use block
The tool_result block must be in a user-role message and its tool_use_id must match the id of the corresponding tool_use block
Each tool_result must include the full input_schema of the tool that was called
The tool_result content must be a JSON object, never a plain string
rationale: A tool_result block is returned in a user message and its tool_use_id must reference the id of the tool_use block the assistant emitted. It is a user turn (representing the tool's output), not assistant. It does not carry the input_schema, and its content may be a string or a list of content blocks.
referenceText: Return tool results in a user message using a tool_result content block whose tool_use_id matches the id from the assistant's tool_use block.

stem: A developer is consuming a streaming (SSE) Messages response and wants to reliably reconstruct the final message and detect errors. Which events should their handler explicitly account for? (Select 3.)
options: error events (for example overloaded_error), which can arrive mid-stream
schema_validation events, which the API emits when input_schema checks fail
message_delta events, which carry top-level changes such as stop_reason and usage
cache_write events, which stream each token written to the prompt cache
content_block_delta events, which carry incremental text or input_json deltas
rationale: A streaming response delivers text/JSON incrementally via content_block_delta, communicates final stop_reason and usage via message_delta, and can surface error events (such as overloaded_error) mid-stream that the client must handle. There are no schema_validation or cache_write SSE event types; validation errors return as ordinary HTTP errors and caching metrics appear in usage.
referenceText: Streaming events include message_start, content_block_start/delta/stop, message_delta, message_stop, ping, and error events such as overloaded_error.

stem: Before running a large job, a developer wants to verify how many input tokens a prompt (including tools and system prompt) will consume, without paying for generation. Which approach is correct?
options: Read the usage.input_tokens field from a prior response and assume it is unchanged
Set max_tokens to 0 in a normal Messages request and inspect the usage block
Divide the total character count of the prompt by four to estimate tokens
Call the /v1/messages/count_tokens endpoint with the same model, system, messages, and tools
rationale: The token counting endpoint accepts the same fields as Messages (model, system, messages, tools) and returns the input token count without generating a response. Reusing an old usage figure ignores prompt changes; max_tokens of 0 is invalid; the chars/4 heuristic is only a rough estimate and does not account for tools or special tokens.
referenceText: Use the count tokens endpoint to determine the number of input tokens a request would use, including messages, system prompt, and tools.

stem: A developer submits a job to the Message Batches API and needs to programmatically know when to fetch results. What is the correct way to determine that the batch is ready?
options: Keep the HTTP connection open; the batch endpoint blocks until all requests finish
Poll the batch's processing_status until it becomes "ended", then retrieve the results file
Wait exactly 24 hours, since every batch completes on a fixed schedule
Subscribe to a webhook, which is the only supported completion signal for batches
rationale: Batches process asynchronously; the developer polls the batch object's processing_status until it reaches "ended", then downloads results via the results URL. The request does not block, batches can finish well before the 24-hour target, and polling (not a mandatory webhook) is the standard retrieval pattern.
referenceText: A batch's processing_status moves to ended when processing completes; retrieve results from the results_url once the batch has ended.

stem: A developer enables prompt caching and wants to confirm from the API response that caching is actually working across requests. Which usage fields are the correct signals to inspect? (Select 2.)
options: cache_hit_ratio, a percentage the API returns per request
cache_creation_input_tokens, which is nonzero on the request that writes the cache
cache_ttl_remaining, the seconds left before the cache expires
cache_read_input_tokens, which is nonzero on later requests that hit the cache
cached_output_tokens, which counts generation reused from the cache
rationale: The usage object reports cache_creation_input_tokens (tokens written to the cache on the first call) and cache_read_input_tokens (tokens served from the cache on subsequent calls). Comparing these across requests confirms caching. cache_hit_ratio, cached_output_tokens, and cache_ttl_remaining are not fields the API returns; caching applies to input, not output.
referenceText: Responses include usage.cache_creation_input_tokens and usage.cache_read_input_tokens to indicate cache writes and hits.

stem: A tool implementation throws an exception at runtime. When returning the outcome to the model, what is the recommended way to let Claude recover gracefully within the same conversation?
options: Omit the tool_result entirely so the model retries the tool automatically
Return the exception in an assistant text message instead of a tool_result
Return a tool_result block with is_error set to true and a message describing the failure
Set stop_reason to "tool_error" in the next request to signal the failure
rationale: A tool_result block supports is_error: true with descriptive content, allowing Claude to see the failure and adjust (retry, ask for input, or explain). Omitting the tool_result breaks the required tool_use/tool_result pairing. The error belongs in the user-role tool_result, not an assistant message, and clients do not set stop_reason on requests.
referenceText: If a tool call fails, return a tool_result block with is_error set to true and an explanation so the model can respond appropriately.

stem: A developer wants to force Claude to always call a specific tool named get_weather during a test, regardless of the model's own judgment. Which configuration achieves this?
options: Set tool_choice to {"type": "auto"} and describe the tool as required
Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}
Remove all other tools from the tools array so only get_weather remains
Set tool_choice to {"type": "any"} with a system prompt naming get_weather
rationale: tool_choice of type "tool" with a name forces that specific tool to be called. "auto" leaves the decision to the model; "any" forces some tool but not a specific one. Removing other tools narrows options but still lets the model answer without a tool unless tool_choice forces it.
referenceText: tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces Claude to use the specified tool; "any" forces use of any tool; "auto" lets the model decide.

stem: During load testing, a developer intermittently receives HTTP 529 responses from the Messages API. What is the correct interpretation and handling?
options: The request body is malformed; fix the JSON before retrying
The API key lacks permission; rotate credentials before retrying
The rate limit was exceeded; wait for the reset window shown in the headers
The API is temporarily overloaded; retry the request with exponential backoff
rationale: 529 is an overloaded_error indicating the service is temporarily overloaded; the correct response is to back off and retry with exponential backoff. 400 signals a malformed request, 401/403 signal auth/permission problems, and 429 (not 529) signals rate limiting.
referenceText: A 529 overloaded_error means Anthropic's API is temporarily overloaded; clients should retry with exponential backoff.

stem: A developer's Messages request fails with HTTP 429. Which action best follows Anthropic's guidance for handling this error?
options: Immediately retry in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Downgrade to a smaller model, since 429 is model-specific
Respect the retry-after header and retry with exponential backoff and jitter
Split the prompt into two requests, since 429 means the payload is too large
rationale: 429 is a rate_limit_error; the response includes a retry-after header, and clients should wait and retry with exponential backoff (adding jitter to avoid thundering herd). Tight-loop retries worsen the situation, model choice does not clear a rate limit, and 429 is about request/token rate, not payload size.
referenceText: On a 429 rate_limit_error, use the retry-after header and exponential backoff before retrying.

stem: When building an empirical evaluation to measure whether a prompt change improved a classification task, which practices align with Anthropic's guidance on developing tests? (Select 2.)
options: Rely on a single anecdotal example that looked good in the playground
Hold the test set fixed and grade the new and old prompts against the same cases
Change the model, prompt, and temperature simultaneously so improvements compound
Prefer many realistic, representative test cases over a few hand-picked easy ones
Use only exact-string matching, since it is valid for every task type
rationale: Sound evals use a fixed, representative test set so changes are compared apples-to-apples, and favor a volume of realistic cases over anecdotes. A single playground example is not evidence; changing multiple variables at once prevents attributing gains; and exact-match grading suits only some tasks (others need model-graded or code-graded checks).
referenceText: Build evals with representative test cases, keep them consistent across variants, and choose grading methods appropriate to the task.

stem: A developer notices Claude returned a response with stop_reason "refusal" and no useful content. What does this indicate?
options: The API rejected the request schema; the tools array must be corrected
The model declined to generate the content for safety reasons; the request should be revised rather than blindly retried
The response was truncated by max_tokens and should be re-requested with a higher limit
The prompt cache expired mid-request and the call must be resent unchanged
rationale: stop_reason "refusal" means the model declined to continue generation for safety reasons; the productive step is to review and revise the request, not retry it unchanged. A schema problem returns a 400, truncation is "max_tokens", and cache expiry does not produce a refusal stop_reason.
referenceText: The refusal stop_reason indicates the model declined to generate a response for safety reasons.

stem: A developer wants reproducible outputs while debugging a prompt so that repeated runs are as consistent as possible. Which setting most directly reduces output variability?
options: Set top_p to 1.0 and leave temperature unset
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to converge
Set temperature to 0 to make sampling as deterministic as possible
Enable prompt caching so identical prompts return identical text
rationale: Lowering temperature to 0 minimizes randomness in sampling, giving the most consistent outputs for debugging (though not a strict guarantee). top_p of 1.0 does not reduce randomness, max_tokens only bounds length, and prompt caching reuses input tokens for cost/latency but does not force identical generations.
referenceText: Lower temperature values produce more deterministic, consistent outputs; temperature 0 is near-deterministic.

stem: When debugging extended thinking, a developer wants to pass the model's prior reasoning back in a multi-turn tool-use loop. What must they preserve about each thinking block?
options: Only the human-readable text of the thinking block, with the signature stripped
The thinking block converted into a system prompt string
Nothing; thinking blocks must always be discarded before the next turn
The thinking block including its signature field must be passed back unmodified
rationale: When continuing a conversation that used extended thinking with tool use, prior thinking blocks (including their signature, which validates integrity) must be passed back unmodified. Stripping the signature or altering the block breaks validation, converting it to a system prompt is incorrect, and the blocks are required (not discarded) in the tool-use continuation.
referenceText: Preserve thinking blocks and their signature when passing them back in subsequent turns during tool use.

stem: A Messages request fails validation because the messages array has two consecutive assistant-role messages. Which rule does this violate?
options: Every message must contain at least one tool_use block
Messages must alternate between user and assistant roles
The first message must always be from the assistant
Assistant messages may not contain more than one content block
rationale: The Messages API requires roles to alternate between user and assistant; two assistant messages in a row is invalid (consecutive same-role blocks should be merged). Tool_use blocks are not required in every message, conversations start with a user turn, and assistant messages may contain multiple content blocks.
referenceText: Messages must alternate between user and assistant roles; consecutive messages of the same role are not allowed.

stem: A developer is debugging a Claude Code workflow and wants deterministic, automated checks to run at defined points (for example, formatting a file after it is edited). Which Claude Code features are appropriate to configure for this? (Select 2.)
options: Streaming SSE events emitted by the Messages API
Hooks, which run shell commands on events such as after a tool call
The Message Batches API processing_status field
A CLAUDE.md file to give the agent persistent project context and conventions
tool_choice set to "any" in the global settings
rationale: Claude Code hooks execute shell commands on lifecycle events (e.g., PostToolUse) for deterministic automation like formatting, and CLAUDE.md supplies persistent project context and conventions the agent follows. SSE streaming, the Batches API status field, and tool_choice are Messages API concepts, not Claude Code configuration mechanisms.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks run user-defined shell commands at lifecycle events; CLAUDE.md provides persistent project context.

stem: A developer testing an MCP server wants to confirm which callable capabilities Claude can invoke. In MCP, which primitive represents functions the model can execute (as opposed to read-only context or reusable templates)?
options: Resources, which provide read-only contextual data identified by URIs
Prompts, which are reusable, user-triggered message templates
Tools, which are model-invocable functions the server exposes
Sampling, which lets the server request completions from the client
rationale: In MCP, tools are the executable, model-invocable functions. Resources are read-only context addressed by URI, prompts are reusable templates typically surfaced to users, and sampling is a server-to-client request for completions, not something the model directly calls as an action.
referenceText: MCP tools are executable functions a model can invoke; resources supply read-only data; prompts are reusable templates.

stem: A developer submits a tool_use turn where the model's generated input is missing a field the tool requires. Which mechanism is designed to catch and communicate this class of problem?
options: The stop_sequences parameter, which validates tool arguments
The tool's input_schema (JSON Schema) with required fields defines valid inputs the model is guided to produce
The metadata field on the request, which enforces argument types
The system prompt, which is the only place required fields can be declared
rationale: A tool's input_schema is a JSON Schema (including a required list) that defines the shape of valid inputs and guides the model to produce conformant tool_use inputs. stop_sequences and metadata do not validate arguments, and required fields belong in the input_schema, not solely the system prompt.
referenceText: Each tool defines an input_schema in JSON Schema format specifying properties and required fields for its inputs.

stem: A developer wants to reduce the risk of an autonomous Claude Code agent making unintended file edits while they review its intended approach first. Which Claude Code capability directly supports this test-and-review pattern?
options: Removing the CLAUDE.md file so the agent has no context to act on
Setting temperature to 0 in the Messages API
Disabling streaming so no partial output is applied
Plan mode, which has the agent propose a plan without making changes until approved
rationale: Plan mode lets Claude Code analyze and present a plan without executing edits until the developer approves, which is ideal for reviewing intended changes before they land. Deleting CLAUDE.md removes helpful context rather than adding a safety gate, and temperature/streaming settings do not govern whether edits are applied.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude Code research and propose an approach without making changes until you approve the plan.

stem: A developer's request returns HTTP 400 with an invalid_request_error. According to Anthropic's error guidance, what is the correct debugging response?
options: Retry immediately with exponential backoff, since 400 is a transient server error
Rotate the API key, since 400 indicates authentication failure
Fix the request itself (for example a malformed field or invalid parameter); retrying it unchanged will fail again
Wait for the rate-limit window to reset before resending the identical request
rationale: A 400 invalid_request_error means there is a problem with the request format or content; it is a client error and must be corrected before it can succeed. It is not transient (so backoff-retry is futile), not authentication (that is 401), and not rate limiting (that is 429).
referenceText: A 400 invalid_request_error indicates an issue with the format or content of the request that must be corrected.

stem: A team is building a content-moderation pipeline that must classify roughly 5 million short user comments per day into 'allowed' or 'flagged'. The taxonomy is simple, latency per request must be minimal, and per-token cost dominates the budget. Which Claude model tier is the most appropriate default choice?
options: The Haiku tier, because it offers the lowest cost and fastest responses for high-volume, well-scoped tasks
The Opus tier, because only the most capable model can be trusted with any classification decision
The Sonnet tier, because it is the only tier that supports structured output for classification
A different model tier for every comment, chosen at random to balance load
rationale: Haiku is Anthropic's fastest and most cost-efficient tier, ideal for high-volume, latency-sensitive, well-scoped tasks like simple classification. Opus is the most capable but costliest and slowest, over-provisioned for a binary taxonomy. Sonnet is not uniquely required for structured output (all tiers support tool use/JSON). Random tier selection is not a sound engineering strategy and ignores cost/latency goals.
referenceText: Models overview — choosing a model by capability, speed, and cost

stem: A developer wants to reuse a large system prompt across many requests with prompt caching. Their system prompt for Claude Sonnet is only about 500 tokens. What will happen when they add a cache_control breakpoint to it?
options: The content will not be cached because it is below the minimum cacheable prefix length for that model
The content will be cached, and every request will still receive the full cache-write surcharge
The request will be rejected with a validation error for using cache_control on system content
The cache will be created but will silently expire after exactly one second
rationale: Prompt caching enforces a minimum cacheable prefix length (about 1024 tokens for Sonnet/Opus tiers, 2048 for Haiku tiers). A 500-token prefix is below the threshold, so it will not be cached. It does not raise an error—cache_control is valid on system content—and the cache TTL is minutes (default 5), not one second.
referenceText: Prompt caching — minimum cacheable prompt length

stem: An analytics team has 80,000 non-urgent summarization requests to run overnight and wants to minimize cost. They do not need results in real time. Which approach best matches this workload?
options: Submit the requests through the Message Batches API to receive a 50% token discount with asynchronous processing
Send all 80,000 requests concurrently to the synchronous Messages API to force parallel discounts
Use extended thinking on every request, since more thinking tokens reduce total cost
Disable streaming and set max_tokens to 1 to make each summary cheaper
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, non-time-sensitive workloads and provides a 50% discount on input and output tokens with asynchronous processing (typically within 24 hours). The synchronous Messages API offers no volume discount for concurrency. Extended thinking adds tokens and cost. Setting max_tokens to 1 would truncate summaries, not produce usable output.
referenceText: Message Batches API — 50% cost savings for asynchronous batches

stem: A developer enables extended thinking and sets max_tokens to 4096. Which constraint must the thinking budget_tokens value satisfy?
options: budget_tokens must be at least 1024 and strictly less than max_tokens
budget_tokens must equal max_tokens so all output is reasoning
budget_tokens must be exactly 512 regardless of the max_tokens value
budget_tokens is ignored because thinking length is fixed by the model
rationale: Extended thinking requires budget_tokens of at least 1024, and because thinking tokens count toward the response, budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens to leave room for the final answer. It cannot equal max_tokens (no room for the visible answer), is not fixed at 512, and is not ignored—the developer sets it explicitly.
referenceText: Extended thinking — setting the thinking budget

stem: An application must call a specific validation tool named 'validate_address' on every request and never respond with free text on that turn. Which tool_choice setting enforces this?
options: {"type": "tool", "name": "validate_address"}
{"type": "auto"}
{"type": "none"}
{"type": "any", "name": "validate_address"}
rationale: tool_choice {"type": "tool", "name": ...} forces the model to call that specific named tool. {"type": "auto"} lets the model decide whether to use any tool. {"type": "none"} prevents all tool use. {"type": "any"} forces some tool but does not take a name field and does not pin a specific tool.
referenceText: Tool use — forcing tool use with tool_choice

stem: While streaming a response that includes a tool call, a client needs to reconstruct the tool's JSON input incrementally. Which streaming delta type carries the partial tool input?
options: content_block_delta events of type input_json_delta
message_delta events of type tool_input
content_block_start events of type text_delta
ping events that embed the full tool arguments
rationale: Tool input arrives as content_block_delta events with type input_json_delta, whose partial_json fragments are concatenated to form the tool's JSON input. message_delta carries top-level message changes (like stop_reason), not tool input. content_block_start signals a block's beginning, not incremental JSON. ping events are keep-alives and carry no arguments.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — input_json_delta for tool use

stem: A developer is estimating the savings of prompt caching for a workload with a large, stable context reused across many requests. Relative to base input token price, what does a cache read (cache hit) approximately cost?
options: About 10% of the base input token price
About 125% of the base input token price
Exactly the same as the base input token price
About 50% of the base output token price
rationale: Cache reads (hits) cost about 10% (0.1x) of the base input token price, which is what makes caching valuable for repeated large contexts. The ~125% figure is the cache-write surcharge for the default TTL, not the read cost. Reads are not full price, and cache pricing is based on input token rates, not output rates.
referenceText: Prompt caching — pricing for cache writes and reads

stem: A response comes back with stop_reason set to 'max_tokens'. What does this indicate, and what is the appropriate handling?
options: The output was truncated because it hit the max_tokens limit; increase max_tokens or continue the generation
The model finished a complete answer normally; no action is needed
The model refused the request for safety reasons; rephrase the prompt
A tool call is pending; return a tool_result and continue
rationale: stop_reason 'max_tokens' means generation was cut off at the token cap and the output may be incomplete; raise max_tokens or continue. 'end_turn' indicates a natural, complete stop. A safety refusal surfaces differently ('refusal'). A pending tool call is signaled by stop_reason 'tool_use', which is what requires returning a tool_result.
referenceText: Messages API — stop_reason values

stem: In a Messages API request, which components of the prompt prefix can carry a cache_control breakpoint for prompt caching? (Select 3.)
options: The tools array
The system prompt
The messages array
The model identifier field
The stop_reason field
rationale: Cache breakpoints can be placed on the tools definitions, the system prompt, and messages content—these form the cacheable prefix in the order tools, then system, then messages. The model identifier is a scalar routing field, not cacheable content, and stop_reason is a response field, not part of the request prefix.
referenceText: Prompt caching — what can be cached (tools, system, messages)

stem: Which statements about the Message Batches API are correct? (Select 2.)
options: Batches are processed asynchronously, typically completing within 24 hours
It provides a 50% discount on input and output tokens compared to standard requests
Every request in a batch returns a live server-sent-events stream
It guarantees sub-second per-request latency for real-time use
rationale: The Batches API processes requests asynchronously (typically well within the 24-hour window) and applies a 50% token discount. It is not a streaming interface—results are retrieved after processing—and it is explicitly not for real-time, low-latency needs, so no sub-second guarantee exists.
referenceText: Message Batches API — asynchronous processing and pricing

stem: A chat feature generates long responses, and users complain the UI feels frozen while waiting, and some requests hit an HTTP client timeout. Which optimization most directly addresses both symptoms?
options: Enable streaming so tokens render incrementally and the connection stays active
Switch to the Batches API so the responses arrive overnight
Set temperature to 0 so the model produces output faster
Remove the system prompt to reduce total latency to near zero
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens incrementally via SSE, improving perceived responsiveness (time-to-first-token) and keeping the connection active, which mitigates client timeouts on long generations. The Batches API is asynchronous and unsuitable for interactive chat. Temperature affects sampling, not throughput. Removing the system prompt does not eliminate generation latency.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — incremental responses and timeouts

stem: A developer relies on prompt caching but notices cache misses when requests are spaced several minutes apart during quiet periods. What is the most likely cause given default caching behavior?
options: The default cache lifetime is about 5 minutes and is refreshed on each hit, so gaps longer than the TTL expire the entry
Cache entries are permanent, so the misses must be caused by a bug in the SDK
Caches are shared across all customers, so another account overwrote the entry
The cache only stores output tokens, so input-heavy prompts are never cached
rationale: The default cache TTL is about 5 minutes and refreshes each time the cached prefix is hit; if requests are spaced beyond the TTL, the entry expires and the next call is a miss (a longer 1-hour TTL is available as an option). Caches are not permanent, are scoped to the account, and cache the input prefix, not output tokens.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache lifetime and TTL

stem: An agent uses extended thinking together with tool use across multiple turns. To preserve the model's reasoning continuity when sending back a tool_result, what must the developer do with the assistant's thinking blocks?
options: Pass the prior assistant turn's thinking blocks (with their signatures) back unmodified in the conversation history
Strip all thinking blocks before replying, since they are internal and must never be resent
Rewrite the thinking blocks into a shorter summary to save tokens before resending
Move the thinking blocks into the system prompt so they persist across turns
rationale: When continuing a tool-use loop with extended thinking, the previous assistant turn's thinking blocks (including their signatures) must be returned unmodified so the model can preserve its reasoning. Stripping, summarizing, or relocating them breaks signature verification and reasoning continuity.
referenceText: Extended thinking — preserving thinking blocks with tool use

stem: A product needs deep multi-step reasoning on a small number of hard legal-analysis requests where quality matters far more than cost or speed. Which model-selection reasoning is most appropriate?
options: Choose the Opus tier, accepting higher cost and latency in exchange for the strongest reasoning capability
Choose the Haiku tier, because the cheapest model is always the correct default for any task
Choose the Haiku tier and raise max_tokens, since more output tokens compensate for lower capability
Avoid choosing a tier and let the model pick its own size at inference time
rationale: For low-volume, high-difficulty reasoning where quality dominates, the Opus tier is the right trade-off despite higher cost and latency. Haiku optimizes for speed and cost, not peak reasoning; raising max_tokens does not add capability. Callers select the model explicitly—the model does not choose its own tier.
referenceText: Models overview — matching model tier to task difficulty

stem: A developer wants the model to answer directly and be prevented from calling any of the provided tools on a particular request. Which tool_choice value achieves this?
options: {"type": "none"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "auto"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "answer"}
rationale: {"type": "none"} disables tool use so the model must answer in text. {"type": "any"} forces the model to use some tool. {"type": "auto"} (the default when tools are present) lets the model decide. {"type": "tool"} forces a specific named tool—the opposite of preventing tool use.
referenceText: Tool use — controlling tool use with tool_choice: none

stem: A nightly batch job sends the same large 30,000-token reference document as context for tens of thousands of requests. Which combination best reduces cost?
options: Use the Batches API for the 50% discount and add a cache_control breakpoint so the shared document is cached across requests
Use only the synchronous Messages API and resend the document each time at full price
Truncate the reference document to under 1024 tokens so caching is forced to apply
Enable extended thinking so the model memorizes the document and stops needing it
rationale: Prompt caching can be combined with the Batches API: the batch discount lowers token cost while caching the stable 30k-token prefix avoids repeatedly paying full input price for it. Resending at full price wastes money. Truncating below the minimum defeats the purpose and loses information. Extended thinking does not persist documents across separate requests.
referenceText: Prompt caching — combining caching with batch processing

stem: A developer wants to cache several distinct segments of a prompt (tools, a long system prompt, and a large document in the first user message) independently. What is the approximate limit on cache breakpoints they can define in a single request?
options: Up to 4 cache_control breakpoints
Exactly 1 cache_control breakpoint per request
Unlimited breakpoints, one per content block
Exactly 10 cache_control breakpoints
rationale: A request may define up to 4 cache breakpoints, letting you cache separate prefixes such as tools, system, and message content independently. It is not limited to one, not unlimited, and the documented maximum is 4, not 10.
referenceText: Prompt caching — up to four cache breakpoints

stem: When consuming a streamed Messages response over SSE, which event signals that the entire message is complete and the stream will end?
options: message_stop
content_block_stop
message_delta
message_start
rationale: message_stop is the final event indicating the whole message is finished. content_block_stop ends a single content block, not the message. message_delta carries top-level updates like stop_reason and usage but is not the terminal event. message_start opens the stream.
referenceText: Streaming Messages — event sequence and message_stop

stem: A developer enables extended thinking and also wants to force the model to always call a specific tool with tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": ...}. What happens, and what is the supported configuration?
options: Forced tool choice is not supported with extended thinking; only tool_choice auto or none may be used
Both work together, and forcing a specific tool improves thinking quality
Extended thinking silently disables itself so the forced tool can run
Forced tool choice works only if temperature is also set to 0
rationale: Extended thinking with tool use supports only tool_choice {"type": "auto"} (default) or {"type": "none"}; forcing a specific tool (or 'any') is not compatible. The features do not silently disable each other, and extended thinking additionally requires temperature to remain at its default of 1, not 0.
referenceText: Extended thinking — tool_choice restrictions with tool use

stem: A developer profiles a request and finds the cacheable prefix is being written on nearly every call rather than read. Relative to base input pricing, what is the approximate cost of a cache write for the default 5-minute TTL, and what does this imply for the caching strategy?
options: A write costs about 125% of base input; caching only pays off when the same prefix is read enough times to offset the write surcharge
A write is free, so caching always saves money even with a 100% miss rate
A write costs about 10% of base input, identical to a read, so write/read balance is irrelevant
A write costs about 200% of base input for the 5-minute TTL, so short TTLs should be avoided entirely
rationale: A cache write for the default (5-minute) TTL costs about 25% more than base input (~1.25x), while reads cost ~10%. Caching pays off only when a written prefix is read enough times to recover the write premium; a workload dominated by writes/misses can cost more than not caching. The ~2x figure applies to the optional 1-hour TTL write, not the 5-minute default.
referenceText: Prompt caching — cache write surcharge vs. read savings

stem: A team runs a content-moderation pipeline that labels millions of short user comments per day as "safe" or "flagged." The task is simple pattern matching that smaller models handle accurately, and per-request latency plus per-token cost dominate their bill. Which model tier best fits this workload?
options: Claude Haiku, the fastest and most cost-effective tier for high-volume, well-scoped tasks
Claude Opus, because the most capable tier should be used for every production task
Claude Sonnet, because classification always requires a mid-tier model
Whichever model advertises the largest context window, regardless of task complexity
rationale: Haiku is Anthropic's fastest, lowest-cost tier and is the recommended choice when a task is simple and high-volume with strong latency/cost sensitivity. Opus is the most capable but most expensive tier and is overkill for simple classification. Sonnet is not universally 'required' for classification; the 'always' framing is a giveaway. Context window size is irrelevant for short comments and does not drive tier selection here.
referenceText: Haiku is the fastest and most cost-effective model, suited to high-volume, simpler tasks; Opus is the most capable for complex reasoning.

stem: You are enabling prompt caching on a Messages API request by attaching a cache_control block to a large system prompt. What value must the block's `type` field be set to?
options: "ephemeral"
"persistent"
"static"
"long_term"
rationale: Prompt caching is enabled by adding a `cache_control` block with `"type": "ephemeral"` at the desired cache breakpoint. The other values are not valid cache_control types; caching is inherently short-lived (a 5-minute default TTL refreshed on use), which is why the type is named 'ephemeral' rather than 'persistent', 'static', or 'long_term'.
referenceText: To cache a prefix, add "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"} to the content block that ends the cacheable prefix.

stem: A data team must summarize 500,000 archived support tickets overnight. Results are not needed in real time, and the team wants to minimize cost. Which approach on the Anthropic platform is most appropriate?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously at a 50% discount on token cost
Send 500,000 synchronous Messages API requests as fast as possible to finish quickly
Use extended thinking on every request to reduce the number of requests needed
Enable streaming so that partial results arrive faster for each ticket
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant, asynchronous workloads and offers roughly a 50% cost reduction versus standard requests, making it ideal for overnight bulk summarization. Firing hundreds of thousands of synchronous requests is costlier and rate-limit constrained. Extended thinking adds tokens/latency and does not reduce request count. Streaming improves perceived latency for a single request but provides no cost savings and does not fit bulk async work.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously, offering a 50% cost discount and returning results within 24 hours.

stem: You enable extended thinking on a model that uses a fixed thinking budget and set `budget_tokens` in the thinking configuration. What is the required relationship between `budget_tokens` and `max_tokens`?
options: `budget_tokens` must be less than `max_tokens`, since the thinking budget is drawn from the overall output allowance
`budget_tokens` must be greater than `max_tokens` to reserve room for reasoning
`budget_tokens` must equal `max_tokens` exactly
`budget_tokens` is independent of `max_tokens` and has no ordering constraint
rationale: The thinking budget is part of the model's output token allotment, so `budget_tokens` must be set below `max_tokens`; the remaining tokens are available for the visible response. It cannot exceed or equal max_tokens in a way that leaves no room for the answer, and it is not independent of it. The minimum budget is 1024 tokens.
referenceText: budget_tokens must always be less than the max_tokens specified, because the thinking tokens count toward your output token limit.

stem: While consuming a streaming (SSE) response from the Messages API, which event type carries the incremental chunks of generated text as they are produced?
options: content_block_delta
message_start
content_block_stop
message_stop
rationale: Incremental text (and input_json for tool calls) arrives via `content_block_delta` events, each containing a delta such as a `text_delta`. `message_start` opens the stream with an initial message shell, `content_block_stop` signals a block is complete, and `message_stop` ends the stream; none of these carry the streamed text fragments.
referenceText: Each content block delta event contains a delta (for example text_delta) that appends incremental text to the current content block.

stem: Your application reuses a large, unchanging tool schema and system prompt on every request and you want to maximize prompt-cache hit rates. Which statements about how prompt caching works are correct? (Select 2.)
options: The cache matches on an exact prefix, so cached content should be placed at the beginning and kept byte-for-byte stable across requests
Cache reads are billed at a fraction of the base input token price, making reuse of a stable prefix cheaper than resending it uncached
Any change late in the prompt invalidates earlier cached prefixes, so dynamic content should be placed before the static content
Cached prefixes persist indefinitely until you explicitly delete them via an API call
Prompt caching automatically compresses the cached tokens so they no longer count toward the context window
rationale: Prompt caching is prefix-based: a cache hit requires the beginning of the prompt to match exactly, so stable content belongs first and must not change. Cache reads are billed at about 0.1x the base input rate, so reusing a stable prefix is much cheaper than resending it. Placing dynamic content before static content is wrong—it breaks the shared prefix and prevents hits (static should come first). Caches are ephemeral with a short TTL, not indefinite or manually deleted. Cached tokens still count toward the context window; caching does not compress context.
referenceText: Prompt caching references the longest previously cached prefix; put static content first and dynamic content last, and cache reads cost a fraction of base input tokens.

stem: A workload sends a short static system prompt of only a few hundred tokens on every request and wants to cache it to cut input costs. Why will a cache breakpoint on that prompt not produce cache hits?
options: The prompt is below the minimum cacheable prefix length, so it is too short to be cached
System prompts can never be cached; only tool definitions are cacheable
Prompt caching requires the Batch API, which this request does not use
Caching only applies to output tokens, not to system-prompt input tokens
rationale: Prompt caching enforces a minimum cacheable prefix length (roughly 1024 tokens for many models, and higher for others); a few-hundred-token prompt is too short to cache and silently produces no cache hit. System prompts, tools, and messages are all cacheable, so the 'only tool definitions' claim is false. Caching works on standard Messages API requests and is not Batch-exclusive, and it applies to input prefix tokens, not output.
referenceText: Cacheable prompts must meet a minimum length; prefixes shorter than the model's minimum will not be cached and cache_creation_input_tokens stays 0.

stem: You have defined three tools and, for one specific step, you want to guarantee that Claude calls the `get_weather` tool rather than replying with text or choosing another tool. How should `tool_choice` be configured?
options: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}` to force that specific tool
Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "auto"}` and let the model decide which tool to use
Set `tool_choice` to `{"type": "none"}` so no other tool can be chosen
Omit `tool_choice`, since specifying tools already forces the first one listed
rationale: `tool_choice` with `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}` forces the model to invoke that exact tool. `auto` lets the model decide (no guarantee), `none` prevents any tool call entirely, and omitting `tool_choice` defaults to auto—listing tools does not force the first one.
referenceText: tool_choice can be set to {"type": "tool", "name": ...} to force use of a specific tool, auto to let the model decide, any to require some tool, or none to disable tool use.

stem: A nightly enrichment job runs the same 8,000-token instruction-and-schema prefix across 200,000 documents and is not latency-sensitive. Which two optimizations most directly reduce token cost for this job? (Select 2.)
options: Submit the requests through the Message Batches API for the asynchronous batch discount
Apply prompt caching to the large stable prefix so repeated prefix tokens are billed at the reduced cache-read rate
Enable extended thinking on every request to shorten the output
Switch to streaming responses to lower total token consumption
Raise `max_tokens` to a very high value so fewer requests are needed
rationale: The Batch API gives an asynchronous cost discount that suits this non-latency-sensitive bulk job, and prompt caching lets the repeated 8,000-token prefix be billed at the much cheaper cache-read rate—these compound. Extended thinking adds reasoning tokens and increases cost/latency. Streaming changes delivery timing, not total tokens or price. Raising max_tokens only sets an upper bound on output; it does not reduce request count or cost and can increase it.
referenceText: Prompt caching and the Message Batches API can be combined to reduce cost for repetitive, latency-tolerant workloads.

stem: You use extended thinking together with tool use in a multi-turn agent loop. After Claude returns a thinking block and a tool_use block, what must you do when sending the tool_result back?
options: Include the original thinking block(s) unmodified in the assistant turn along with the tool_use, so the reasoning context is preserved
Strip out the thinking blocks before sending the next request to save tokens
Convert the thinking block into a user message so the model can read it
Replace the thinking block with a summary you write yourself
rationale: When combining extended thinking with tool use, the thinking blocks (including their signatures) must be passed back unmodified alongside the tool_use so the model can continue its reasoning correctly. Stripping, converting to a user message, or rewriting the thinking content breaks the reasoning chain and can cause errors or degraded results.
referenceText: When using extended thinking with tool use, you must pass the thinking blocks back to the API unmodified, preserving them in the assistant turn.

stem: A response from the Messages API ends with `"stop_reason": "max_tokens"`. What does this indicate and what is the appropriate response?
options: The output was truncated because it hit the `max_tokens` limit; raise the limit or continue the generation if a complete answer is required
The model finished its answer naturally and no action is needed
The model decided to call a tool and you should execute it
The request violated a stop sequence and must be resubmitted with different content
rationale: `stop_reason: max_tokens` means generation was cut off at the configured output limit, so the content may be incomplete; you typically increase max_tokens or continue. `end_turn` (not max_tokens) means a natural completion, `tool_use` signals a tool call, and `stop_sequence` indicates a stop sequence was hit—each is a distinct stop_reason value.
referenceText: stop_reason of max_tokens indicates the response was truncated at the max_tokens limit; end_turn, tool_use, and stop_sequence represent other completion states.

stem: Your prompt-cached prefix is being reused by requests that arrive roughly every two minutes throughout the day. Regarding the default cache lifetime, which statement is accurate?
options: The default cache TTL is about 5 minutes and is refreshed each time the cached prefix is used, so steady 2-minute traffic keeps it warm
The default cache lasts 24 hours regardless of usage
The cache expires immediately after a single read and cannot be reused
The cache never expires unless the model version changes
rationale: The default cache TTL is approximately 5 minutes, and each cache hit refreshes the timer, so requests arriving every 2 minutes keep the entry alive. It is not a fixed 24-hour lifetime, it survives beyond a single read, and it does expire (a longer 1-hour TTL option exists but is not the default).
referenceText: The default cache has a 5-minute time to live, refreshed each time the cached content is used; a 1-hour TTL is also available.

stem: A chat product wants to reduce the time users wait before seeing the first words of Claude's reply, even though total generation time is unchanged. Which optimization directly addresses this?
options: Enable streaming so tokens are delivered incrementally, lowering time-to-first-token and improving perceived latency
Increase `max_tokens` so the model has more room to respond
Switch to the Message Batches API for asynchronous processing
Add more few-shot examples to the prompt so the model responds faster
rationale: Streaming sends output token-by-token via SSE, so users see text almost immediately, reducing time-to-first-token and improving perceived responsiveness even if total time is the same. Raising max_tokens does not speed delivery. The Batch API is asynchronous and increases latency. Adding examples lengthens the prompt and typically increases, not decreases, latency.
referenceText: Streaming responses improves perceived latency by delivering tokens as they are generated rather than waiting for the full completion.

stem: A team is building a long-horizon coding agent that must plan across many files, use multiple tools, and reason through complex trade-offs where accuracy matters more than per-token cost. Which model tier is the best default starting point?
options: Claude Opus, the most capable tier for complex reasoning and agentic workflows
Claude Haiku, since agents should default to the cheapest tier
Whichever tier is oldest, to maximize compatibility
A tier chosen at random per request to balance cost
rationale: Opus is the most capable tier and is recommended for complex, multi-step reasoning and agentic tasks where quality dominates cost. Defaulting agents to the cheapest tier sacrifices the reasoning quality such workloads need. Choosing by age or randomizing tiers are not principled selection strategies. Teams may later downshift specific sub-tasks to Sonnet/Haiku after evaluation.
referenceText: Opus is the most intelligent model, best for complex, agentic, and reasoning-heavy tasks; smaller tiers trade capability for speed and cost.

stem: An application must feed very large documents to Claude while controlling cost and staying within the context window. Which two strategies are sound? (Select 2.)
options: Cache the large, unchanging document prefix with prompt caching so repeated requests reuse it at the cheaper cache-read rate
Retrieve and include only the passages relevant to the user's query instead of pasting the entire corpus every time
Send the full corpus on every request to guarantee completeness, regardless of token cost
Set `max_tokens` higher than the model's context window to expand how much input it can read
Disable streaming so the model can process more input tokens
rationale: Prompt caching a stable document prefix reduces the cost of reusing large context, and retrieval (sending only relevant passages) keeps requests within the window and cheaper. Sending the full corpus every time wastes tokens and can exceed limits. `max_tokens` governs output length and cannot expand the context window. Streaming affects output delivery, not input capacity.
referenceText: For long context, provide only relevant material and use prompt caching to reuse stable large inputs efficiently; max_tokens controls output, not the context window.

stem: Which statement accurately characterizes the Message Batches API's processing guarantees?
options: Batches are processed asynchronously and complete within 24 hours, with results retrievable for a limited retention period after completion
Batches return synchronously within a few seconds like standard requests
Batches are guaranteed to finish in exactly one hour every time
Batch results are deleted the instant the batch finishes, so they must be streamed live
rationale: The Batches API is asynchronous and targets completion within 24 hours, and results remain available to retrieve for a retention window (29 days) after processing. It is not synchronous or a few seconds, there is no exact one-hour guarantee, and results are not deleted immediately—there is a retrieval window.
referenceText: Message batches are processed asynchronously and typically complete within 24 hours; results are available to download for 29 days.

stem: A summarization endpoint frequently returns long, rambling answers, which increases both latency and output-token cost. Which change most directly reduces output cost and latency while keeping answers on target?
options: Constrain the output—prompt for a concise format and set a sensible `max_tokens`—so the model produces shorter responses
Raise `max_tokens` so the model has more freedom to elaborate
Enable extended thinking to make responses longer and more detailed
Add the full source document again in the assistant turn to reinforce context
rationale: Output tokens drive both latency and cost, so instructing the model to be concise and capping max_tokens directly reduces generation length. Raising max_tokens permits longer, costlier output. Extended thinking adds reasoning tokens and length. Re-adding the source document increases input tokens and does nothing to shorten output.
referenceText: Reducing the number of generated output tokens—via concise prompting and appropriate max_tokens—lowers latency and cost, since output tokens are the primary latency driver.

stem: When integrating Claude into production code, why is it a best practice to reference a specific pinned model version (for example, a dated model ID) rather than only a floating alias?
options: A pinned version keeps behavior stable and reproducible, so your evaluations and outputs do not shift unexpectedly when a new model is released
Pinned versions are always cheaper than aliases
Aliases cannot be used with the Messages API at all
Only pinned versions support tool use and streaming
rationale: Pinning a specific model version gives deterministic, reproducible behavior so upgrades happen on your schedule after re-evaluation, rather than silently changing when an alias advances. Pinned versions are not inherently cheaper, aliases are valid on the Messages API, and features like tool use and streaming are not exclusive to pinned IDs.
referenceText: Use specific model version strings for production stability; migrate deliberately and re-test when adopting a newer model.

stem: A voice assistant needs the lowest possible response latency for short, conversational replies, and the tasks are not reasoning-intensive. Beyond choosing the fastest model tier, which additional technique most improves responsiveness for the end user?
options: Stream the response so the assistant can begin speaking as soon as the first tokens arrive
Submit each turn through the Message Batches API
Enable extended thinking with a large token budget on every turn
Set `max_tokens` to the maximum the model allows for every reply
rationale: Streaming lets the assistant start producing audio from the earliest tokens, cutting perceived latency for real-time conversation. The Batch API is asynchronous and the opposite of low-latency. Extended thinking adds reasoning time and delays first output. Maxing out max_tokens invites longer generations, increasing latency.
referenceText: For latency-sensitive applications, choose a faster model and use streaming to reduce time-to-first-token; avoid unnecessary thinking budgets.

stem: You are placing cache breakpoints to optimize a request that contains, in order: tool definitions, a long static system prompt, and a short user question that changes every call. Which two practices maximize cache reuse? (Select 2.)
options: Order content from most static to most dynamic, keeping the changing user question at the end after the cached prefix
Put the cache_control breakpoint at the end of the stable content (after tools and system prompt) so the shared prefix is as long as possible
Place the breakpoint before the tool definitions so only the changing question is cached
Move the changing user question to the top of the prompt so it is cached first
Add a fresh random string to the top of every request to keep the cache from growing stale
rationale: Because caching matches an exact prefix, static content (tools, system prompt) should come first with the breakpoint at the end of that stable region, maximizing the reusable prefix while the volatile question trails at the end. Caching only the changing question, moving it to the top, or injecting a random string at the top all destroy the shared prefix and prevent cache hits.
referenceText: Structure prompts with static content first and a cache breakpoint after it; dynamic content should come last to preserve a long cacheable prefix.

stem: A team runs about 5 million short product-review classifications per day, each sorting text into one of three fixed categories. Per-item latency is not critical, but total cost must be minimized. Which model is the best fit?
options: Claude Haiku 4.5
Claude Sonnet 5
Claude Opus 4.8
Claude Fable 5
rationale: Haiku 4.5 is the fastest and most cost-effective tier and is well-matched to simple, high-volume classification, so it minimizes cost without sacrificing quality on an easy task (and pairs naturally with the Batches API for a further 50% discount). Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, and Fable 5 are all far more capable and more expensive than a three-way label task requires; Fable 5 in particular is the most expensive tier ($10/$50 per MTok) and is intended for the most demanding reasoning, making it the worst cost fit.
referenceText: Claude Haiku 4.5 — Fastest and most cost-effective model for simple tasks.

stem: A developer adds cache_control to a large, unchanging system prompt, but usage.cache_read_input_tokens stays 0 across many repeated requests. What is the most likely cause?
options: A datetime.now() value is interpolated into the system prompt prefix on every request
The cache_control TTL was set to "1h" instead of the default 5 minutes
max_tokens is set too low for the responses
The code calls client.messages.create instead of the beta messages endpoint
rationale: Prompt caching is a prefix match: any byte change anywhere in the prefix invalidates the cache from that point on, so a per-request timestamp in the system prompt makes every request a unique prefix and reads never hit. A longer TTL only changes how long entries live (writes still cache), a low max_tokens affects output length not cache reads, and caching does not require the beta endpoint — none of those zero out cache reads.
referenceText: Prompt caching is a prefix match. Any change anywhere in the prefix invalidates everything after it.

stem: A nightly job submits 40,000 independent summarization requests that have no real-time latency requirement, using the Message Batches API. Which statement about handling the results is correct?
options: Results can return in any order, so each result must be matched to its input by custom_id
Results are guaranteed to return in the same order the requests were submitted
Batches cost roughly 2x standard pricing in exchange for asynchronous processing
Each batch is limited to 1,000 requests, so the job must be split into 40 batches
rationale: Batch results arrive in any order and must be keyed by the custom_id supplied per request, never by position. The Batches API costs 50% of standard pricing (not 2x) and supports up to 100,000 requests or 256 MB per batch, so 40,000 requests fit in a single batch.
referenceText: Results arrive in any order — key by custom_id, never by position.

stem: A request that previously used thinking={"type":"enabled","budget_tokens":8000} is being migrated to claude-opus-4-8. If the code is left unchanged, what happens, and what is the correct fix?
options: It returns a 400 error; replace it with thinking={"type":"adaptive"} and control depth via output_config.effort
It works unchanged because budget_tokens is still supported on Opus 4.8
The budget_tokens field is silently ignored, so no change is needed
It returns a 400 error; the fix is to lower budget_tokens below max_tokens
rationale: On Opus 4.8 (and 4.7), thinking={"type":"enabled", "budget_tokens": N} is removed and returns a 400. The migration is to adaptive thinking, using output_config.effort to control how much the model thinks. It is not silently ignored, is not still supported, and lowering budget_tokens does not help because the whole enabled/budget_tokens shape is rejected.
referenceText: thinking: {type: 'enabled', budget_tokens: N} is removed and rejected with a 400 on Opus 4.8/4.7; use thinking: {type: 'adaptive'}.

stem: A non-streaming request sets max_tokens=100000 and intermittently fails with client HTTP timeouts. What is the recommended fix?
options: Stream the request and collect the result with get_final_message()/finalMessage()
Raise the client's max_retries so timed-out requests are retried more often
Lower max_tokens to about 100 so the response returns quickly
Move the workload to the Message Batches API to avoid timeouts
rationale: Large max_tokens on a non-streaming request risks SDK HTTP timeouts; the documented fix is to stream (values above ~16K should stream) and use the SDK's final-message helper. Raising retries just re-incurs the same timeout, dropping max_tokens to 100 truncates the intended output, and switching to Batches changes the request's latency and semantics rather than fixing the timeout on this synchronous call.
referenceText: Stream for max_tokens above ~16K; non-streaming requests hit SDK HTTP timeouts at high max_tokens.

stem: To decide whether a prompt fits the context window, a developer estimated its size with the tiktoken library. Why is this unreliable for Claude, and what is the correct approach?
options: tiktoken is OpenAI's tokenizer and miscounts Claude tokens; use the count_tokens endpoint with the target model
tiktoken is accurate for Claude as long as the encoding is set to cl100k_base
Character count divided by 4 gives an exact token count for any model
Token counts are identical across all providers, so any tokenizer is fine
rationale: tiktoken is OpenAI's tokenizer and undercounts Claude tokens (by ~15-20% on typical text, more on code or non-English), so it is wrong for Claude. The accurate approach is the count_tokens endpoint, called with the same model ID you will use for inference, since token counts are model-specific. A characters/4 heuristic is only a rough estimate and providers' tokenizers differ.
referenceText: Do not use tiktoken — it undercounts Claude tokens. Use the count_tokens endpoint with the same model ID you'll use for inference.

stem: An agentic coding workflow on Opus 4.8 produces excellent results at output_config.effort="max" but burns more tokens per task than the budget allows. What is the best first adjustment to reduce token spend while keeping quality high?
options: Lower output_config.effort (for example to "high"), which reduces thinking depth and token usage
Set temperature=0 to make the model more deterministic and terse
Disable thinking entirely with thinking={"type":"disabled"}
Switch back to thinking with an explicit budget_tokens ceiling
rationale: effort is the primary lever for the intelligence-versus-token-spend tradeoff; stepping down from max to high (often the sweet spot) cuts token usage while retaining strong quality on coding/agentic work. temperature is rejected with a 400 on Opus 4.8, disabling thinking sacrifices quality on reasoning-heavy tasks and can cause reasoning to leak into the visible response, and budget_tokens is removed on Opus 4.8 (400).
referenceText: effort controls thinking depth and overall token spend; lower effort means fewer, more-consolidated tool calls and less spend — high is often the sweet spot.

stem: Which statements about prompt-cache pricing are correct? (Select 2.)
options: A 5-minute ephemeral cache write costs about 1.25x the base input token price
A cache read costs about 0.1x the base input token price
A 1-hour TTL cache write costs the same as a 5-minute write
A cache read costs the same as an uncached input token
Writing to the cache is free
rationale: A 5-minute ephemeral write costs ~1.25x base input price and a cache read costs ~0.1x base input price — the economics that make caching pay off after a couple of reads. A 1-hour TTL write costs 2x (not the same as 5-minute), a cache read is far cheaper than uncached input (not the same), and writes are not free.
referenceText: Cache reads cost ~0.1x base input; writes cost 1.25x for 5-minute TTL, 2x for 1-hour TTL.

stem: Many requests share a large fixed preamble (instructions plus few-shot examples) but end with a different final question each time. Which practices maximize cache hits across these requests? (Select 2.)
options: Place the cache_control breakpoint at the end of the shared preamble, before the varying question
Serialize tool definitions in a stable, deterministic order across requests
Interpolate the current timestamp into the system prompt header on each request
Put the varying question before the shared preamble
Prepend a fresh UUID to the system prompt on every request
rationale: The breakpoint belongs at the end of the shared portion so every request reads the same cached prefix; placing it after the varying question would write a new entry each time and never read. Deterministic tool ordering keeps the position-0 tools block byte-identical so it caches across requests. Timestamps, UUIDs, and putting volatile content before the stable prefix all change the prefix and defeat caching.
referenceText: Put the breakpoint at the end of the shared portion; keep stable content (frozen system prompt, deterministic tool list) first and volatile content after the last breakpoint.

stem: For a long-running agent, a developer must choose between compaction and context editing to manage a growing transcript. Which statement correctly distinguishes them?
options: Compaction summarizes earlier context into a summary block; context editing clears (removes) stale tool results or thinking blocks
Both features summarize the transcript in exactly the same way
Context editing summarizes older turns, while compaction deletes them without summarizing
Compaction runs entirely client-side, while context editing runs server-side
rationale: Compaction condenses earlier context into a server-side summary block (which must be preserved on subsequent requests), whereas context editing prunes stale tool results and thinking blocks by removing them rather than summarizing. They are not identical, the summarize/remove roles are not reversed, and both operate server-side via the beta Messages endpoint.
referenceText: Context editing clears old tool results or thinking blocks; it is not compaction, which summarizes.

stem: You are building a document-Q&A feature that sends the same 40,000-token knowledge base on every request, followed by a short user question that changes each time. You want to use prompt caching to reduce latency and cost. Where should you place the `cache_control` breakpoint?
options: On the final user question, so the most recently changed content is cached
On the last block of the static knowledge base, so everything up to and including it is cached and reused
On the system prompt only, because caching cannot be applied to user-turn content
On every content block, so each is cached independently
rationale: Prompt caching caches the entire prefix up to and including the block marked with `cache_control`. Placing the breakpoint on the last static (knowledge base) block caches the large unchanging prefix while leaving the changing question after it as an uncached suffix. Caching the changing question (option 0) would invalidate the cache every request. Caching cannot be limited to system prompts—user and tool content are cacheable too (option 2). Marking every block (option 3) is unnecessary and you are limited to a small number of breakpoints; the prefix model means one well-placed breakpoint suffices.
referenceText: Prompt caching — how cache_control marks a prefix boundary

stem: A developer's prompt mixes a contract to analyze, formatting instructions, and few-shot examples in one undifferentiated block, and Claude sometimes confuses the instructions with the contract text. Which change most directly addresses this per Anthropic's prompt engineering guidance?
options: Wrap each distinct part in descriptive XML tags such as <contract>, <instructions>, and <examples>
Move the entire prompt into the system parameter instead of the user turn
Increase max_tokens so Claude has room to separate the sections itself
Set temperature to 0 so the model treats all sections identically
rationale: Anthropic recommends XML tags to clearly delimit different parts of a prompt (data vs. instructions vs. examples), which reduces the chance Claude mistakes one for another. Moving everything into the system prompt (option 1) does not separate the intermingled parts. max_tokens (option 2) controls output length, not input structure. Temperature (option 3) affects sampling randomness, not section delineation.
referenceText: Use XML tags to structure your prompts

stem: Claude is inconsistently classifying support tickets into your five categories despite a detailed instruction paragraph. According to Anthropic's guidance, which technique will most reliably improve accuracy and consistency?
options: Add several diverse, well-labeled examples showing input tickets mapped to the correct category
Raise the temperature so the model explores more category options
Repeat the category definitions three times in the prompt
Ask Claude to classify each ticket twice and keep the second answer
rationale: Multishot (few-shot) prompting—providing 3-5 diverse, relevant examples—is Anthropic's recommended way to improve accuracy and enforce consistent output structure for classification tasks. Higher temperature (option 1) increases variability, worsening consistency. Repetition (option 2) adds tokens without teaching the mapping. Classifying twice (option 3) does not give the model the demonstrations it needs to learn boundaries.
